author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Abraham and his entire family were circumcised, receiving the sign of the covenant and setting themselves apart from families who had no part in this matter. It was a demonstration of implicit obedience; he did exactly as God commanded without questioning why. He obeyed simply because God told him to, and he did so immediately—on that very same day. Sincere obedience does not delay. The rituals of God's covenant remind us that we are guilty and imperfect. They point us to our need for atonement and to the promised Savior, teaching us to trust in Him. They show us that without spiritual renewal and the putting away of our worldly desires, we cannot be in a relationship with God. However, we must remember that true circumcision is of the heart, by the Spirit (Romans 2:28-29). In both the old and new eras, many have had the outward signs of faith without ever being truly transformed by the Holy Spirit.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Pharaoh believed Israel was lost in the wilderness and would be easy to capture. But God declared that He would be honored through Pharaoh’s defeat. Since all people are created for God's glory, those who refuse to honor Him willingly will eventually bring Him honor through His justice. Events that seem to lead to the church's destruction often result in the ruin of its enemies instead. While Pharaoh was driven by malice and revenge, he was actually fulfilling God's plans for him. Although he had let Israel go for good reasons, he later regretted his decision. God uses the rage and envy of men against His people to become their own torment. Those who set their sights on heaven and follow Christ should expect to be attacked by Satan’s temptations and threats, as he does not let anyone leave his service without a fight.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: This is a list of the cities of Judah. Interestingly, Bethlehem is not mentioned here, though it would later become the city of David and the birthplace of our Lord Jesus. Bethlehem was small among the thousands of Judah (Micah 5:2), and at this time, it was so insignificant that it wasn't even counted as a primary city.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Abigail believed David would eventually rule Israel and held his character in high regard. She saw his proposal as an honor and a blessing, despite his current struggles. With great humility and respect for the customs of her time, she agreed to marry him, willing to share in his hardships. In the same way, those who join themselves to Christ must be willing to suffer with Him now, confident that they will one day reign with Him.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Solomon’s dream was extraordinary. While his body slept, his soul was strengthened, allowing him to receive a divine vision and make a wise choice. In the same way, God encourages us toward happiness by promising that He will provide what we truly need when we pray. The fact that Solomon made such a wise choice while asleep reflects God's grace working within him. Deeply aware of his own limitations, he pleaded, 'I am but a little child.' Truly wise people are well-acquainted with their own weaknesses and careful not to trust in themselves. Solomon asked God for wisdom—something we should all pray for (James 1:5) to help us in our specific roles and daily life. God accepts those who value spiritual blessings over earthly wealth. Because Solomon’s prayer was sincere, God gave him more than he asked: not only unparalleled wisdom but also wealth and honor. When we prioritize wisdom and grace, other blessings often follow or help us endure without them. We find spiritual blessings through persistent prayer and earthly blessings by trusting God's will for them. Solomon received wisdom because he asked for it, and wealth because he didn't.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Christ calls His people to listen to His voice. If you call Him Master or Lord, be willing and obedient. Listen to His teachings, His laws, and the guidance of His Spirit; hear and take it to heart. We must listen to Christ today, as this window of opportunity will not last forever. Hearing Christ’s voice is synonymous with believing in Him. A hard heart is the root of all distrust in the Lord. The mistakes of others should serve as warnings for us not to follow in their footsteps; Israel's complaints were recorded for our instruction. While God does not experience human-like passions, He is deeply opposed to sin. Sin always has consequences, and God's warnings are as certain as His promises. We should be aware of the tendencies in our own hearts that lead us away from Him. There is a rest prepared for believers—a place of eternal refreshment that begins in this life and is completed in the next. This is the rest that God offers.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Every faithful servant of God can echo Christ’s words. Their teaching isn't their own discovery but comes from God's Word through the Holy Spirit. Amidst the world's many arguments, anyone who truly seeks to do God's will will recognize whether a teaching is from God or merely human. Only those who reject the truth will fall into dangerous errors. It was just as consistent with the purpose of the Sabbath to restore health to the suffering as it was to perform outward rituals. Jesus taught that His actions should be judged by the spiritual meaning of God's law. We shouldn't judge based on outward appearances, but rather by inner worth and the gifts of God's Spirit.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Everyone serves the master they obey—whether it's the sinful desires that lead to death or the spiritual obedience that comes from a new life in Christ. The apostle was glad that these believers obeyed the gospel from the heart, which reshapes them like a new mold. Just as metal becomes a new vessel when melted and recast, a believer becomes a new creation. There is a profound difference between the freedom of a mind and spirit in Christ and the state of spiritual slavery. A true Christian serves a rightful Lord who is also a Father, making the believer a child and heir by grace. Sin’s control lies in being a willing slave to it, rather than just struggling against it as a power you hate. Those who now serve God were once slaves to sin.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: Christians are called out of the world's spirit and values; called to higher, eternal things in heaven; called from sin to Christ and from vanity to holiness. All this is according to God's purpose and grace. Any honor and glory for this transformation belong to God alone. Just as God starts the work of grace in us, He is the one who continues and completes it. We shouldn't trust in ourselves or our own strength, but in God alone. His mercy is the source of all our blessings—mercy shown not just to the struggling, but to the guilty. Peace follows mercy, and from that peace comes love—Christ's love for us and our love for one another. The apostle’s prayer is that believers would be filled with these things, not just satisfied with a little. No one is excluded from the gospel's invitation except those who stubbornly shut themselves out. This applies to all believers, both weak and strong. Those who hold the truth of salvation must defend it earnestly, but not with anger or hostility. Lying for the truth is wrong, and scolding is no better. We should defend the truth with patience and courage, not by making others suffer. We must resist those who would corrupt the faith—those who sneak in quietly. The most dangerous are those who use God's grace as an excuse to sin boldly, ignoring that the purpose of grace is to deliver us from sin and bring us back to God.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: External privileges and outward profession cannot save someone from God's judgment if they turn toward unbelief and disobedience. The fact that the Israelites perished in the wilderness despite experiencing daily miracles shows that no one should take their status for granted. Similarly, many angels fell because of pride, dissatisfied with the roles God gave them. They are now kept for the final judgment, and sinful humanity will not escape it either. The destruction of Sodom serves as a stark warning to flee from the worldly desires that battle against the soul (1 Peter 2:11). God remains the same holy and just Being today. We should live with reverence and avoid sin. Nothing matters more than heart-felt obedience to Christ; only the renewal of our souls by the Holy Spirit can protect us from the consequences of rejecting God. Consider the fall of the angels—no position or status is enough to save someone who persists in sin. If they fell, how much more should we be careful?

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: False teachers are like dreamers who deeply harm their own souls and the souls of others. They are often restless and rebellious, forgetting that authority is established by God. The story of the dispute over Moses' body suggests that Satan wanted to lead Israel into idolatry by revealing his burial place, but he was stopped. This reminds us to avoid insults during disagreements and to defend those who belong to God. Often, the enemies of faith live in contradiction even to basic moral principles, behaving more like animals than the wise people they claim to be. Their faults lie in their choices and desires, not just their understanding. It is a tragedy when people who claim to follow God actually oppose Him in their hearts and lives. God will address this in His own timing, without harming the sincere along with the hypocrites. It is sad when someone starts their journey in the Spirit but ends it in the flesh. They are described as 'twice dead'—once in their natural state and now again through their hypocrisy. Like dead trees that provide no fruit, they are destined for judgment. Just as waves are a terror to sailors until they reach port, false teachers bring chaos but will eventually face punishment. They may shine briefly like falling stars, but they will end in darkness. The prophecy of Enoch confirms that Christ’s coming to judge was predicted since ancient times. This will be a glorious event. Notice how often the word 'ungodly' is used; while some might mock the term today, it is essential in the language of the Holy Spirit. Every harsh word against others will be accounted for on judgment day. These restless people are never satisfied with their lives, making their own desires their only law. Those who chase sinful appetites are most prone to uncontrolled anger. From the beginning, God's people have warned of the consequences of such a path. Let us avoid those who follow it and only follow others as they follow Christ.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: People driven only by their senses distance themselves from Christ and His church, following sinful paths instead. This is far worse than disagreements over opinions or styles of worship. Those without the Spirit of holiness do not belong to Christ. True faith is holy; it works through love, purifies the heart, and overcomes worldly temptations. Our prayers are most effective when guided by the Holy Spirit, with sincerity and faith. Looking forward to eternal life helps us resist the traps of sin and worldly desires. We must look out for one another, offering correction with both faithfulness and kindness. We should treat the weak with tenderness, while warning the stubborn of the consequences of their actions. In all things, we must hate what is evil and stay far away from anything that leads to sin or darkness.

text: People driven only by their senses distance themselves from Christ and His church, following sinful paths instead. This is far worse than disagreements over opinions or styles of worship. Those without the Spirit of holiness do not belong to Christ. True faith is holy; it works through love, purifies the heart, and overcomes worldly temptations. Our prayers are most effective when guided by the Holy Spirit, with sincerity and faith. Looking forward to eternal life helps us resist the traps of sin and worldly desires. We must look out for one another, offering correction with both faithfulness and kindness. We should treat the weak with tenderness, while warning the stubborn of the consequences of their actions. In all things, we must hate what is evil and stay far away from anything that leads to sin or darkness.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)

Those without the Spirit of holiness do not belong to Christ. True faith is holy; it works through love, purifies the heart, and overcomes worldly temptations. We must look out for one another, offering correction with both faithfulness and kindness. We should treat the weak with tenderness, while warning the stubborn of the consequences of their actions. In all things, we must hate what is evil and stay far away from anything that leads to sin or darkness.

Those without the Spirit of holiness do not belong to Christ. True faith is holy; it works through love, purifies the heart, and overcomes worldly temptations. Our prayers are most effective when guided by the Holy Spirit, with sincerity and faith. Looking forward to eternal life helps us resist the traps of sin and worldly desires. We must look out for one another, offering correction with both faithfulness and kindness. We should treat the weak with tenderness, while warning the stubborn of the consequences of their actions. In all things, we must hate what is evil and stay far away from anything that leads to sin or darkness.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: God is both able and willing to keep us from falling and to present us as blameless in His glorious presence. This isn't because we were never at fault, but because of God's mercy and the Savior's sacrifice. All true believers are safe in His hands; He has not lost a single one. Although our mistakes currently bring us fear and sorrow, the Redeemer has promised to present His people as faultless. Where there is no sin, there will be no sorrow, and where there is perfect holiness, there will be perfect joy. Let us look to Him who sustains and grows the work He has started in us. In that day, our hearts will know a joy that this world cannot offer, and God will rejoice over us. To Him who designed this perfect plan and will bring it to completion be all glory, majesty, and power, now and forever. Amen.

author: Armor & Light (Revised)
text: God is both able and willing to keep us from falling and to present us as blameless in His glorious presence. This isn't because we were never at fault, but because of God's mercy and the Savior's sacrifice. All true believers are safe in His hands; He has not lost a single one. Although our mistakes currently bring us fear and sorrow, the Redeemer has promised to present His people as faultless. In that day, our hearts will know a joy that this world cannot offer, and God will rejoice over us. To Him who designed this perfect plan and will bring it to completion be all glory, majesty, and power, now and forever. Amen.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3283 of the world; 721 BC.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3287 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3294 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The name Tobias means 'Good God.' In the ancient Latin version and according to St. Ambrose, he is called Tobis, while the Greek and Syriac versions call him Tobit. These versions and the Hebrew text provide genealogies that don't entirely align. The Greek edition describes the book as the 'transactions of Tobit,' son of Tobiel, listing a lineage through Adouel and Galael from the tribe of Naphtali. This lineage identifies him as one taken captive during the reign of the Assyrian king Shalmaneser (Enemessar) from the city of Thisbe in Galilee. Tobit speaks of himself in the first person, saying he walked in truth and justice throughout his life—a common narrative style used by Nehemiah and other prophets. The location mentioned as 'beyond' or 'behind' likely refers to the west, assuming the observer is facing east, placing Sephet to the north.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This refers to King Shalmaneser. After the defeat of King Hoshea in 3283 (see 4 Kings 17:6), Tobit remained remarkably constant in his faith, even though he was wealthy and lived among a wicked population.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Referring to his relatives; the Greek version adds that they accompanied him to Nineveh.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit was relatively young when he began managing his own affairs, as his parents had already passed away. The Greek version emphasizes his youth while still in the land of Israel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Most of his tribe had turned away from the faith, though some, like Ananias and Jonathan, remained loyal to God. The Greek version describes how the tribe of Naphtali abandoned Jerusalem—the city chosen for all Israel to offer sacrifice—and instead sacrificed to the idols of Baal. Despite this, Tobit would frequently travel alone to Jerusalem for the major feasts. Differences between the Greek, Syriac, and Chaldee versions suggest the possibility of two original sources, perhaps written by both the elder and younger Tobias, though this theory hasn't been proven.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit followed the laws of tithing, giving a portion to those in need such as strangers or orphans. He notes that he followed the instructions of his grandmother, Deborah, as he had been orphaned at a young age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In Jewish tradition, men married young, though the specific age wasn't fixed. While other versions call him Tobias, the father is consistently referred to as Tobit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nineveh, also known as Ninus, was a city large enough to accommodate an entire enslaved tribe.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit avoided eating foods that were forbidden by Jewish law or that had been sacrificed to idols.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit served as a steward or purchasing agent for the palace, managing provisions until the king's death. Some versions may emphasize his importance, noting he traveled into Media and entrusted Gabael (Gabelus) with a significant sum.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The city of Rages, often subject to earthquakes, served as a spring residence for Parthian kings. Tobit was well-compensated for his work there.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In those times, it was common for people to make use of funds that had been left in their care. The ancient Latin version specifies a deposit of ten talents in gold.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Shalmaneser reigned for fourteen years. After he died, his son Sennacherib succeeded him. During Sennacherib’s reign, trade routes were often disrupted by war, preventing Tobit from traveling into Media. This period was marked by an increased hostility toward Israel, particularly after Sennacherib’s failed expedition into Palestine.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Despite losing his official position, Tobit continued his charitable work under Sennacherib. Some versions suggest these events may have actually started under his predecessor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit risked his life to bury the bodies of those slain by Sennacherib, motivated by charity and a belief in the resurrection. This practice reflects the deep respect the faithful have for tombs and the relics of saints. While pagans believed a soul couldn't find rest without a proper burial, Tobit acted out of religious duty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3295 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit followed the law (Deuteronomy 12:12) by hosting feasts and inviting the poor and fellow servants of God. As was common in the East, he reclined while he ate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In the Greek version, this refers to indigent brethren who remember God. Not all had succumbed to idolatry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The fact that Esarhaddon didn't protect the Israelites makes Ahikar's high position suspicious, though the king and his ministers couldn't prevent every murder. It was customary for the ancients to not eat before noon. The Greek version describes Tobit leaping up to take someone into a house before sunset to avoid the Assyrians. If he didn't have help, he would have eaten alone after becoming ritually unclean, which explains why he lay down by the wall instead of entering his house. Both the Greek and Hebrew texts mention he washed before eating.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This version isn't in the Greek or Hebrew. True religious zeal is not repressed by fear, as charity expels fear (1 John 4).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Greek version specifies 'that very night.' Tobit lay down uncovered in the courtyard because touching a dead body made one ritually unclean (Numbers 19:11).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While the Greek uses the term for sparrows or small birds, the Hebrew notes the event happened 'by chance.' Pliny observed that some animals and people sleep with their eyes open, as was the case here. Bird dung, being caustic, caused white specks or skins to form on Tobit's eyes, resulting in a temporary loss of sight. He sought help from physicians but found no relief. Ahikar then supported him until he went to Elymais.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This is omitted in the Greek and Hebrew. St. Ambrose noted that Tobit was more grieved by his inability to help others than by his own blindness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He shows no impatience, even in his distress.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even in punishment, God is merciful and always follows through on His promises.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Scripture sometimes suggests that children are affected by the sins of their parents (Exodus 20:5), and the Church still prays, 'Remember not our offenses, nor those of our parents.' However, children are only truly punished when they follow the wicked examples of their ancestors. If they are innocent, their afflictions serve to test and strengthen their virtue. Ancient pagan thinkers, struggling to explain suffering in seemingly innocent people, hypothesized about sins from another life. This may have been a confused understanding of original sin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Greek version expresses a desire for death over a life full of false reproaches and grief, asking to return to the earth and be freed of this necessity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In the Greek, this is Ecbatana, which was also called Rages. There were two cities in Media named Rages: Raguel lived in one, and Gabael in the other. Some authors mention a Rages near Ecbatana, likely referring to the province.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Asmodeus is interpreted here as 'the fire of Media,' a demon that incites lust and destruction. The Greek and Hebrew versions suggest this demon attacked when husbands first entered the nuptial chamber.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Greek version portrays the servants taunting Sarah, accusing her of suffocating her seven husbands and telling her to follow them in death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Upon hearing these taunts, Sarah was deeply grieved and, according to some interpretations, even considered taking her own life. However, this interpretation is likely groundless and unfair to her character, as she would have known that such a thought was sinful. She prayed at the window, noting she was her father's only daughter and to take her life would bring disgrace upon him. The Hebrew and Syriac versions omit several verses here.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sarah's prayer, which appears differently across several versions, focuses on praising God's name and glory, asking that all His works praise Him forever.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Like Sarah, other holy figures such as Moses, Elijah, Job, and St. Paul have, at times, expressed a desire for death, provided it aligned with God's will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sarah maintained her purity even in captivity, a point she mentioned in prayer not out of vanity, but out of confidence in God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This refers to lascivious acting or dancing, similar to what occurred when Israel turned to the golden calf.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit remembered the money left with Gabael and, in anticipation of his death, gave his son fourteen instructions, modeling the final words of Moses and David. He specifically told Tobias to honor and care for his mother after his father’s death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: St. Augustine marveled at how Tobit, even with his physical eyes closed, could teach his son the spiritual way of life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit requested to be buried in the same tomb as his parents, following the tradition of the patriarchs. This reflects the later Church decree of being buried among one's ancestors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Don’t be generous with what doesn’t belong to you. When giving alms, do it without envy and with a generous heart. This aligns with Daniel's teaching on the spiritual reward of good works.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God values the heart behind the gift more than the amount given.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Acts of charity can lead to a fresh supply of grace, provided they are rooted in faith. Scripture teaches that genuine alms can help deliver the soul from the spiritual 'darkness' or judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Give charity with true love and sincerity (1 Corinthians 13:3).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit instructs his son to marry within his own tribe, following the line of the prophets like Noah. He warns against 'strange' or foreign wives who might lead him away from his heritage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This refers to the downfall of Adam and the rebel angels.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wages should be paid before sunset (Deuteronomy 24:14).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This is the foundational rule of charity: do not do to another what you wouldn't want done to yourself. This maxim was so respected that Alexander Severus had it inscribed on public buildings. Some versions also include warnings against drunkenness and revenge.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be generous with your extra resources and do so without regret.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jews and early Christians provided food for the poor on behalf of the deceased, hoping for their prayers. This custom was common but was eventually restricted by St. Ambrose to prevent abuse. It serves no purpose to pray or give alms for those who are lost, so such works were done for those believed to have died in God's peace. Acts of mercy extend even beyond this life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit concludes his instructions by reminding his son that God is the source of all good, humbling or exalting people as He sees fit. He tells him to always keep these commands in his heart and reveals the location of the silver left with Gabael.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: These verses about disposing of temporal effects are omitted in the Greek versions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit provided a 'handwriting' or contract as a token for the money, as Tobias didn't know Gabael personally. At this point, the narrative switches from the first to the third person.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael appeared as a beautiful young man, concealing his true angelic nature. This prefigured Christ, who truly took on our human nature.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael identifies himself as being from among the children of Israel, looking for work or employment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael notes he has stayed with Gabael, their kinsman.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Greek and Hebrew versions include a mutual greeting between them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: When asked for his lineage, Raphael identifies as being of the family of Azarias and Ananias. This isn't a lie in the traditional sense; angels acting in God's name often take on human identities without hesitation. The name 'Azarias' means 'God's help,' and 'Ananias' means 'God's grace,' which fits Raphael's divine mission as God's healer. Tobias didn't ask if he was an angel, only for his family name.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit recognizes the names and offers the traveler a wage of a drachm a day plus travel expenses. They agreed on the terms, and Tobias prepared for the journey.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Provisions were gathered for the trip, and the dog accompanied Tobias and the angel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While critics might scoff at the mention of a dog, such details are common in great historical accounts of loyalty. Tobit and Raphael stayed at a night station or caravansary, which were common shelters for travelers in the Persian kingdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Scholars have debated the identity of the fish, with some suggesting a Pike or a large sturgeon found in the Tigris. Its gall was known to have medicinal properties for the eyes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This verse is missing in the Greek. Both Tobias and the fish were in the water when the encounter began.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias caught the fish by its gill and brought it to land, a detail that helps identify the fish’s nature.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: These details are omitted in some Hebrew versions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The journey from Nineveh to Rages likely took about 10 to 12 days. They ate some of the fish and traveled together toward Ecbatana.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias asks about the purpose of the fish’s heart, liver, and gall.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God gave these physical items a specific power over evil spirits, forcing those who tried to be like the Most High to submit to lowly creatures. This is similar to how Christ used clay for healing; the remedies were effective because of God’s appointment. The smoke was a sign of the demon's expulsion and the power of prayer.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The gall's medicinal properties for the eyes are well-documented by ancient physicians, countering critics’ objections to this account.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: As they near Rages, Raphael proposes that Tobias marry Sarah, the daughter of Raguel. Since Tobias is her closest relative, the inheritance belongs to him by law. Sarah is described as both beautiful and wise, and the angel promises to speak to her father on Tobias's behalf.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias expressing fear is simply an account of his opinion or what he had heard: that a demon was in love with Sarah and had destroyed her previous seven husbands. While the idea of demons falling in love with humans is generally dismissed now, it was a common belief in the past. In this case, God permitted the demon to strike against those who approached Sarah with impure motives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This refers to the place of the souls (Hades/Sheol) where they were kept before Christ's coming. Tobias feared his death would bring his parents to their graves in grief.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael reminds Tobias of his father’s wish for him to marry within his tribe and tells him not to fear the demon. He instructs him to use the fish's heart and liver on the incense as a remedy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some people are compared to mules—driven by uncontrolled desires. God sometimes allows the wicked to fall victim to their own impulses while giving others time to repent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They were received with joy, though Raguel only knew them as fellow Israelites at first. Sarah greeted them and led them into the house.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raguel noticed the resemblance between Tobias and his cousin Tobit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They confirm that they know Tobit and that he is alive and well. Tobias then identifies him as his father.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Upon hearing of Tobit's blindness, Raguel was deeply moved and wept.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They were given a warm welcome with a great feast. Tobias, however, requested that the matter of his marriage to Sarah be settled before he ate. Raguel truthfully explained that he had given his daughter to seven men, all of whom died on their wedding night. Despite this warning, Tobias insisted, and Raguel agreed, granting them a fatherly blessing. Raguel's honesty is commendable, as many would have hidden such a tragedy to marry off their daughter.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Marriage within the tribe was a sacred duty, especially for an heiress. To refuse such a marriage was considered a disgrace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The agreement was confirmed by a handshake and a written contract, which was a common custom in Media. Such formal writings were almost universal to ensure the legality and conditions of the marriage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sarah was led to a different chamber, possibly to the one where the feast was held, so as not to alarm her given the tragedies of the past.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He joined her in the chamber.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias used the fish's liver as instructed. This act symbolized Christ's Passion, which drives the devil from human hearts. The demon fled to the remote deserts of Egypt, where the angel bound him. While critics may mock this, it represents the reality that evil cannot act without divine permission and that it can be restricted by God's power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Before they slept, Tobias invited Sarah to rise and pray that God would show them mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They praised the God of their fathers, asking that all creation bless Him forever.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias recalled how God created Eve as a helper for Adam, decreeing that man should not be alone.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias declared his sincere and faithful love for Sarah, a sentiment so pure it remains a model for Christian marriage today. St. Augustine referenced this text to show the true purpose of marriage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They prayed for God's mercy and that they might grow old together. They then rested for the night.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Antigipating the worst, Raguel dug a grave during the night.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He wanted to be prepared to bury Tobias secretly so that no one would know of his death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A servant found the couple sleeping peacefully. Raguel, relieved, blessed God. Some versions mention they spent the first three nights in prayer and restraint.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raguel praised God for filling him with joy instead of the tragedy he expected, acknowledging God's great mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raguel ordered the grave to be filled and hosted a fourteen-day feast. He also promised half of his wealth to Tobias immediately, with the other half to be inherited later.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raguel expected the journey would continue to Rages.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias asks Raphael to take a servant and two camels to finish their errand.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While Tobias stayed for the feast, Raphael went to retrieve the money from Gabael.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Concerned for his father, Tobias found a way to show respect to everyone involved while ensuring his business was completed efficiently.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael retrieved the funds and returned for the wedding celebration. Tobias's marriage brought an end to Sarah's long period of distress.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit counted the days and began to worry when his son didn't return, fearing he might have been detained or refused payment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His mother was overcome with grief, fearing her son—the light of her eyes—had met a tragic end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She refused comfort and watched the road every day for her son. This depth of distress reflects the pain of those who feel abandoned by God, while others, like Tobit, continue to hope for light to be restored.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias is mentioned again.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The journey and celebration had taken more time than anticipated. While his parents grew anxious after twenty days, the fourteen-day wedding feast made his return appear delayed. Tobias, feeling his parents’ worry, asked to be dismissed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raguel gave Tobias his wife Sarah along with half of his wealth, including servants and livestock. Sarah was instructed to honor her in-laws as her own parents. As they departed, Edna entrusted her daughter to Tobias as a sacred deposit, asking him not to grieve her. Their departure was marked by mutual blessings and remains a beautiful description of a holy marriage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias traveled back toward Nineveh. To move more quickly, he left the main group behind so he could reach his aging parents sooner.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael suggested they go ahead to prepare the house and relieve his parents' anxiety.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They took the fish's gall with them and were followed by their loyal dog.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit's wife saw them coming.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael assured Tobias that his father's sight would be restored. He instructed him to use the gall on his father’s eyes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The mention of the dog suggests that even small details in scripture can have deep meaning. Writers of history have long recorded the incredible fidelity of dogs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His mother ran to him and wept for joy. Tobit, though blind, stumbled toward the door to meet them. Tobias applied the gall to his father's eyes, and as they smarted, Tobit rubbed them, and his sight was miraculously restored.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The details about the 'hour' of the cure are only in the Vulgate. Scholars debate whether the healing was a natural property of the fish gall or a miraculous revelation of a remedy. Pliny and other ancient writers confirmed that certain fish gall could consume redundant flesh and heal scars on the eyes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit's prayer of praise acknowledges God’s power to both discipline and show mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sarah arrived shortly after Tobias. Tobit’s restoration of sight astonished the people of Nineveh, and he openly confessed God’s mercy before them. He welcomed Sarah with a fatherly blessing, and there was great joy among all his kinsmen.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit consults with his son about paying Raphael his wages, plus a bonus as promised.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias offers Raphael half of all they brought back, acknowledging that the traveler had protected him, healed his wife, retrieved the money, and cured his father. Raphael is invited to depart in health.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael calls the two of them aside for a private conversation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael exhorts them to bless God and openly proclaim His works. He explains that while secrecy is necessary for kings to protect their plans, it is a duty to reveal and honor the works of God. Raphael reveals that when Tobit was burying the dead, even leaving his meals to do so, it was the angel who presented those good works before God. He identifies himself as one of the seven angels who stand before the brightness of God. The two men were terrified and fell prostrate, but Raphael reassured them with a message of peace and returned to heaven. He instructed them to record everything that had happened.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Charity, prayer, and fasting are spiritually connected. Raphael adds that a little given with justice is better than much gained through iniquity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who practice charity and justice will be filled with spiritual life, while those who commit sin are enemies of their own souls.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This doesn't mean the wicked never prosper in this life, but rather that sin is essentially self-destructive in an eternal sense.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael emphasizes that he was present during Tobit's prayers and acts of charity. Angels act as ambassadors and ministers, offering the memorials of our prayers before the Holy One.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Trial and suffering are often allowed for the good so they can grow in virtue and serve as examples to others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This history provides evidence of the protection we receive from our guardian angels and other blessed spirits.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael is one of the seven primary angels mentioned in the Book of Revelation (Apocalypse 1:4). These 'princes of the heavenly court' stand ready to fulfill God's orders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Raphael clarifies that he didn't come of his own accord, but by the will of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Although Raphael appeared to eat and drink, it was a vision. As a spirit, he had no need for food; his 'food' was the constant vision of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After the angel's departure, Tobit and Tobias proclaimed God's wonderful works. Interpreters infer that the book itself was originally composed by these two holy men based on the angel's instruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3299 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit wrote a prayer of exultation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God has the power over life and death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the scattering of Israel among the Gentiles served a purpose: to make God's law known to other nations. Tobit encourages his people to convert with all their heart so that God will turn toward them once more.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God's mercy is based on His character, not on anything the sinner can offer.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit magnifying God's power even before a 'sinful nation' (the Assyrians) suggests that his message of repentance was for all who would listen.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit’s prophecy about Jerusalem refers both to the rebuilding after the exile and to the spiritual Jerusalem—the Church. Although the timing of the city’s destruction and Tobit’s death is debated, he speaks with prophetic certainty about the future restoration of the temple.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jerusalem will eventually be a place where captives find joy and the distressed find love forever.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many nations will come to honor God, bringing presents for the King of Heaven. This prophecy was partially fulfilled by the respect shown to the Jewish temple by foreign monarchs and fully realized in the Church, as the powers of the earth eventually submitted to Christ.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This soil has been carried into distant countries out of reverence, and has been honored with miracles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who hate the holy city will be cursed, but those who love it will be blessed forever.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The children of the just will be gathered together. The Jews became remarkably more faithful after the captivity, displaying great courage even during the persecutions of Antiochus Epiphanes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who have mourned because of their discipline will eventually rejoice in God's eternal glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3341 of the world (663 BC).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit lived to a very advanced age. While versions differ on the exact numbers, he spent his remaining years recovered from blindness, giving alms, and praising God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mention of children/grandchildren.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: (Small numerical detail)

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Before his death, Tobit advised his son to move to Media. He believed Jonah’s prophecy about Nineveh’s destruction would surely come true. He also foretold the scattering of Israel, the burning of the temple, and its eventual glorious rebuilding, when even the Gentiles would turn to fear the Lord and destroy their idols.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This conversion of the nations to the true God was fulfilled in the Church, as the prophets predicted.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The prediction concerning Nineveh's ruin was fulfilled after the people relapsed into their former sins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobit’s final instructions to his son emphasized keeping the law, practicing charity, and living justly. He specifically warned against the consequences of greed and betrayal, referencing the downfall of those who were ungrateful for the kindness shown to them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After his parents' deaths, Tobias moved his family to Ecbatana and lived to an honourable old age, inheriting the wealth of both his and his wife's families.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tobias lived long enough to see several generations of his descendants.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3347 of the world (657 BC).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This opens the story of Arphaxad, king of the Medes, who fortified the city of Ecbatana with massive walls of varied colors. Arphaxad is often identified with Phraortes, a king who greatly embellished his capital.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The architecture focused on massive solidity, with walls seventy cubits high and towers that rose a hundred cubits above the gates, designed to support the kingdom's military and defense.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Arphaxad prided himself on his power, but pride often precedes a fall.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nebuchadnezzar (not the later king of Babylon, but an Assyrian king reigning in Nineveh) rose to oppose him. Known as Saosduchin in some histories, he was a contemporary of King Manasseh of Judah.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The two kings fought in the plain of Ragau. Nebuchadnezzar called for support from many nations, but many refused him, treating his ambassadors with contempt. Enraged, he swore to take revenge on those who defied him. After defeating Arphaxad, he abandoned himself to celebration with his army.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Reference to Jezreel (Esdrelon).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Reference to Goshen (Jesse).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many refused to help because they didn't fear Nebuchadnezzar yet, viewing him as no more than their equal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While those subject to him were wrong to refuse aid, the Jews were under no such obligation. Their cause was especially favored by God because Nebuchadnezzar ambitiously sought universal dominion and even promoted himself as a deity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Year 3348 of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In the spring of the twelfth or eighteenth year of his reign.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The king held a secret council to plan his revenge.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He decided that all who had refused him should be destroyed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes, the second-in-command of the Assyrian army, was chosen to lead the attack.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He was ordered to demand 'earth and water' from the nations—a traditional Persian sign of total submission and ownership.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Provisioning was a priority; he gathered immense amounts of grain to ensure his massive army wouldn't go hungry in the lands he invaded.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The army included a mixed multitude of support roles alongside the soldiers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They traveled from Nineveh toward Cappadocia, home to the highest mountains in the region.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They moved through Melothus and Cilicia, pushing toward the borders of Arabia.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They campaigned from the Chaboras river down to the sea.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes destroyed all who opposed him as he moved toward the borders of Arabia.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He burned the tents and plundered the herds of the Midianites.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The speed of his conquest was remarkable, likely because he sent several lieutenants in different directions at once. He systematically destroyed crops and livestock, and put the young men to the sword.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His progress struck fear into the coastal cities of Sidon and Tyre.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Terrified local kings sent ambassadors to plead for peace, offering themselves as servants to Nebuchadnezzar. Holofernes took their best men for his own forces while being received with honor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They felt it better to submit to slavery than to be slaughtered like their neighbors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They submitted unconditionally to his disposal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They welcomed him with joy and festive torches.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who had fled previous Assyrian invasions consolidated their retreat in Judah.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jews realized that surrendering would not save their temple or their religion. They had recently returned from captivity under King Manasseh and had purified the sanctuary; therefore, they were determined to resist. While some suspect this took place after the Babylonian exile, it likely occurred under Manasseh during his later years after he returned from his own brief captivity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even Samaria was contacted, as the danger was common to all.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They prepared for war just as the harvest ended.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The high priest in Jerusalem ordered the people of Bethulia—a fortress guarding the passage into Judea—to seize the narrow mountain passes. This defensive strategy mirrors the famous resistance of the Spartans at Thermopylae.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The high priest and the senate in Jerusalem took charge of the nation's defense while King Manasseh was potentially still detained in Babylon or occupied with the city’s fortifications.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The entire nation, including women, children, and even livestock, joined in a period of intense prayer and fasting, donning sackcloth and covering themselves in ashes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The priests arrayed the altar itself in sackcloth, creating a spectacle of profound sorrow and petition.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The high priest Joachim and the people continued their fast and offered constant prayers, asking God not to allow their wives and children to be taken as plunder or their sanctuary to be profaned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: These preparations happened before the enemy actually reached them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They displayed their grief and humility through their clothing and actions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They fortified the mountain summits and blocked the plains with ditches and snares.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes was joined by local leaders like the sons of Ammon, who had already submitted.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He demanded to know the origin of the people holding the mountain passes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Although Holofernes wasn't entirely ignorant of the Jews, he spoke of them with contempt, wondering if they were relying on an alliance with Egypt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Achior, a leader of the Ammonites, spoke up to explain the history of the Jewish people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He began by noting their origin among the Chaldeans in Ur.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Achior explained that they left their ancestral home because they refused to follow the idols of the Chaldeans, following instead the God of Heaven. After a time in Mesopotamia, they moved to Canaan and grew wealthy and numerous.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Later, they were oppressed as workers in Egypt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Achior described the plagues on Egypt, the miraculous crossing of the Red Sea, and their eventual conquest of Canaan, where they displaced several powerful nations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God protected them during their journey through the harsh desert.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They defeated all the nations that opposed them during the time of Joshua.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He noted that whenever they were unfaithful to their God, they were defeated and even led into captivity. However, he emphasized that they had recently returned from their scattering and had reclaimed Jerusalem and the mountains. Some scholars suggest this refers to the Babylonian exile, but it also fits the return of Manasseh from Babylon and the subsequent restoration of the temple.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Achior warned the foreigners and mercenaries in Holofernes' army that these people were protected by their God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes and his officers were enraged by Achior’s advice, declaring that their king, Nebuchadnezzar, was the only true god and would soon crush these mountain-dwellers. They resented such bold advice, much like ancient kings who often punished those who spoke inconvenient truths.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes threatened Achior, telling him he would be stationed with the Jews in Bethulia so that he would perish alongside them when the city was taken. He declared that nothing he had spoken would be without effect.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Bethulia is believed to be the fortress of Bethul in the tribe of Simeon, located near the passage into the southern regions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mention of several local leaders in Bethulia.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people of Bethulia comforted Achior, praising him for his courage. They held a feast and spent the night in prayer, calling on the God of Israel for help.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Achior remained with them, joined in their cause and their faith.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jewish fast ended at nightfall, and no further abstinence was required afterward.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'church' mentioned here refers to the synagogue or place of assembly for prayer. Such places were established even during the captivity, as seen in the times of the Maccabees and Esther.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Versions differ on the exact numbers of cavalry and infantry in Holofernes' army, but it's clear his forces were massive and likely grew as local groups joined the campaign.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes moved his army toward the coast, intending to bypass the narrow mountain passes of Ephraim on his way to Egypt. Only the fortress of Bethulia stood in his way and resisted his progress.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The massive scale of the Assyrian army terrified those who saw it, fearing the earth itself couldn't bear their weight. The people of Bethulia prepared their defenses and lit signal fires on their towers, seeking God's help before taking up arms. Holofernes personally reconnoitered the city's defenses and seized its water sources.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some interpret the city's water supply as an aqueduct, which would require the source to be on higher ground or moved through pipes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many local groups, including the Edomites, Tyrians, and Philistines, joined Holofernes as he prepared his siege.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Large detachments of the army were stationed to guard the springs and blockade Bethulia. Holofernes' strategy was a total blockade to avoid losing men in a direct assault, keeping his army fresh for the eventual campaign against Egypt. His forces covered the entire landscape.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The siege lasted for several weeks, and the shortage of water caused many in the city, especially children and the elderly, to faint or die of thirst.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Desperate, some citizens felt it was better to surrender and be slaves than to die of thirst.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They called for the city to be given up for plunder to end their suffering.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They prayed for mercy, even referencing the sins of their ancestors, hoping God would deliver them from death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ozias (Uzziah), the magistrate, encouraged the people to take courage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He requested five more days of endurance, hoping for divine intervention.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people returned to their posts on the walls and towers, living in a state of deep mourning and anxiety.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This verse introduces Judith’s lineage, which scholars have traced through several generations back to the tribe of Simeon. There are some naming variations between the Greek and Vulgate versions, but it’s clear she and her husband were of the same tribe.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith's husband passed away during the harvest season.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He was buried with his ancestors in his own city.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith lived as a widow for over three years, leading a deeply religious life. She stayed in a private chamber on her roof to pray without distraction and donned sackcloth as a sign of her mourning and dedication to God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She fasted every day except on the Sabbaths, new moons, and major festivals. It was Jewish custom to avoid fasting on these days so that the joy of the holiday remained intact.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith was wealthy, inheriting lands and livestock from her husband.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Her reputation for character and virtue was beyond reproach. In those times, as now, such a reputation was considered a beautiful but fragile thing, especially for a woman without a husband's protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: When Judith heard about the people's despair and the rash oath the magistrates had taken to surrender in five days, she sent for the city's leaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith boldly rebuked the magistrates for testing God and trying to set a timeline for His providence. She argued that they should have resisted more firmly, as the safety of the entire nation and the honor of the sanctuary depended on their courage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She reminded them that God cannot be pressured or threatened like a human being; He doesn't owe an account of His plans to anyone.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith noted that, unlike past generations, her people were no longer practicing idolatry, and therefore they should have more confidence in God's protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She warned that surrendering would lead to the profanation of the sanctuary and the plunder of their sacred things—consequences for which the magistrates would be held responsible.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She urged them to show their fellow citizens that their courage was the support for the temple and the altar. She encouraged them to view their current trial as a test of faith, much like the trials of their ancestors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She cited the example of Jacob in Mesopotamia, noting that God’s discipline is meant to examine the heart and instruct those who seek Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: St. Paul likely alluded to this passage (1 Corinthians 10:10) when discussing the punishments inflicted on the unfaithful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith advised them to endure their suffering without the impatience or anger that leads to testing God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ozias acknowledged Judith’s wisdom, noting that her reputation for prudence was well-known from her youth. He explained, however, that the people's extreme thirst had forced the magistrates into their current oath. Based on her age and the number of suitors she still had, Judith was likely in her mid-thirties at this time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith promised to perform an action that would be remembered by all future generations, though she did not yet reveal her plan.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She gave her loyal companion or 'maid of honor' charge over her affairs and told the magistrates to watch at the city gate that night.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She refused to reveal her plan until it was completed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The magistrates departed to their posts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith prayed in her personal oratory, covering herself in ashes and sackcloth. Her prayer coincided with the offering of incense in the Temple in Jerusalem.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith’s prayer begins by praising the justice shown through her ancestor Simeon against the Sichemites. While his father Jacob had condemned the excessive manner of the act, Judith praises the righteous zeal that motivated it—a zeal for protecting family honor and religious purity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Her prayer is poetically structured, describing how God strikes down the prideful and puts the sword into the hands of those who seek His justice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She acknowledges that God has foreseen all things, and that every event unfolds according to His eternal decrees.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nothing can resist God's judgment once it has been determined.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She contrasts the Assyrians’ boastful trust in their physical weapons and cavalry with the power of the Lord, who can bring an end to any war.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This refers to how the Egyptians were unable to pursue Israel during the night at the Red Sea.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith specifically asks for the grace to use her words and her appearance to bring down the Assyrian leaders. While some might question the morality of her strategy, it should be viewed as a wartime stratagem. Her intent was pure: to save her nation without yielding to any sinful proposal. God favored her plan and even enhanced her natural beauty for the mission.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith finished her prayer, put away her mourning sackcloth, and prepared herself for the journey.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She adorned herself in richness and beauty, using perfumes and jewelry (like sandals and necklaces) typically worn by women of high quality to ensure she would captivate the Assyrians.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The early Church fathers highly praised Judith's virtue. While her use of beauty to entice an enemy might seem unusual, she moved forward with the purest intentions to free her country. Her rich attire was not sinful in itself, especially given the necessity of her mission.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She brought her own provisions (wine, oil, and pure bread) to ensure she could follow her religious dietary laws while in the enemy camp.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She left the city with her maid, her appearance completely transformed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The city leaders blessed her as she departed. She moved down the hill and across the valley toward the Assyrian advance guard.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: When stopped, Judith used a clever stratagem, claiming she was fleeing a doomed city to give the Assyrian general, Holofernes, the truth about how to conquer the region. Her words were technically true in a prophetic sense: the city was indeed at the end of its human strength.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Assyrian soldiers were astonished by her beauty and conducted her to Holofernes' tent. People ran from all parts of the camp to see her.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The soldiers felt that any nation that produced such women must be conquered. Holofernes was resting in his inner room behind a canopy or curtain designed to keep out insects—a common luxury for high officers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes came out to meet her, preceded by silver lamps. Judith showed him civil respect by bowing, and his servants lifted her up. Scholars estimate she was about forty years old at this time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes is addressed as king or ruler of all the earth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He assured her of her safety, telling her that no one who submitted to Nebuchadnezzar would be harmed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith promised to tell him the truth, framing her words in a way that the general would interpret favorably but that remained true to her own mission.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She used respectful language, common for speaking to a ruler, a practice seen also in the stories of Joseph and Elisha.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith advised him to trust Achior’s words, confirming that her people are only vulnerable when they sin against God. She claimed that 'sin has taken possession of them,' a statement that was true of the desperate state of the city's leaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She referenced the warnings of the ancient prophets regarding the consequences of infidelity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She described how their extreme thirst was driving them to desperate measures.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She claimed to the Assyrians that the Jews were on the verge of eating forbidden foods to survive, which would surely provoke God's anger.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith claimed the people in Bethulia were planning to consume the sacred first-fruits and tithes reserved for the priests in Jerusalem. She used this to insinuate that they were about to lose God's protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She presented herself as a religious woman who served the God of Heaven day and night, claiming she was there to help Holofernes perform a feat that would astonish the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She requested permission to go out into the valley at night to pray.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She promised to lead Holofernes through Judea to Jerusalem, telling him he would drive the people like sheep and that not even a dog would bark at him. This was deep irony: the people were at peace only after Holofernes was dead, and she indeed brought his head in triumph.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She flattered Holofernes, making him believe he was under the special care of heaven.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The general’s council was pleased by her words, seeing her as a way to easily defeat the Israelites.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes was captivated by Judith's beauty and her speech. He even seemed open to honoring her God, though likely to remove any barriers to her favor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He ordered a table to be prepared for her with his own food and wine as a sign of high honor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith refused his food to avoid participating in meats that had been offered to idols or were religiously forbidden.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He assured her that no one would take advantage of her.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith rested until midnight and then rose for her morning prayer.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She respectfully asked again for permission to go out to pray, establishing a pattern that would eventually allow her to escape the camp unnoticed. Her choice of the fields for prayer was a tactical move.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The guards were ordered not to hinder her. She remained in the camp for three days.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She performed her ritual washings in the camp.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She continued to fast until evening and only ate her own pure food.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: On the fourth day, Holofernes hosted a private feast for his inner circle of servants. He instructed his chief officer, Bagoas (a name commonly used for eunuchs in the Persian court), to persuade the Hebrew woman to join them, feeling it would be a personal disgrace if he could not win her over.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He invited her to be 'merry' and to take on a prominent role in the king's household.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith responded with polite compliments, knowing she could interpret 'merriment' in her own righteous way while the general saw it as an opportunity for indulgence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She adorned herself in her finest ornaments and joined the feast. According to ancient Eastern custom, she reclined on skins on the ground to eat.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes had been waiting for such an opportunity since he first saw her, and he quickly fell into his own trap.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He became exceptionally cheerful and drank more wine than he had ever consumed in a single day.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: As the feast ended, his servants departed and closed the doors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Holofernes was unconscious, overwhelmed by wine.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith was left alone with him, while her companion waited outside as usual. All other servants had left, creating the perfect opportunity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith prayed silently in her heart for the strength to complete her task.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She asked God to strengthen her hands so that she could deliver Jerusalem and fulfill her promise.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She took Holofernes' own sword from where it hung and prepared to strike. While her actions were severe, she was motivated by a divine inspiration to save her people from a cruel enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She took his head and tossed his body to the ground, ensuring everyone would see that the great Assyrian leader had fallen.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She quickly composed herself and left the tent with the head hidden in her bag.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She approached the city gate of Bethulia and called to the watchmen, announcing that God was with Israel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The citizens, hearing her voice, rushed down to open the gate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They were astonished to see her return, as they had already begun to fear the worst.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith loudly proclaimed that God had not withdrawn His mercy and had destroyed their enemies by her hand.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: St. Ambrose noted that the fasting of one woman was enough to overthrow an entire host of drunkards.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith assured the people that the general had not sinned with her and that God's protection had preserved her honor. The people fell down and blessed God for bringing their enemies to nothing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The head of Holofernes was displayed on the city wall.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith instructed the men to take up their arms and make a show of force as if they were going to attack. This would cause the Assyrian guards to rush to Holofernes' tent, find him dead, and be seized with panic.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She also requested that Achior be brought to see the head of the man who had despised Israel. When Achior saw it, he was filled with awe and became a sincere convert to the God of Israel. He requested to be circumcised and joined the Jewish community, a testimony to his profound faith and sincerity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Greek text describes the soldiers moving in companies toward the mountain's ascent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The captains and leaders of the Assyrians, hearing the noise, gathered at Holofernes' tent. They urged his chief steward to wake him, thinking the 'slaves' (the Israelites) were being foolishly bold by coming down to offer battle for their own destruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Assyrians spoke of their enemies with contempt, comparing them to mice coming out of their holes, similar to how the Philistines once spoke of the Israelites.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Bagoas knocked at the door of the inner tent, assuming Holofernes was sleeping with Judith. Receiving no answer, he entered the bed-chamber and found the general’s headless body on the floor. The inner apartment was separated by a fine curtain, a common luxury for Persian kings and high officers. Civility required a modest knock, emphasizing the respect and fear they had for their leader.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Bagoas cried out that the 'slaves' had betrayed them, perhaps suspecting that Judith had managed to smuggle in several of the Jews to help her.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The other captains were alerted by his shouting.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Panic spread through the camp as the news of Holofernes' death broke. Rushing out in fear and trembling, the soldiers fled in every direction, including the Moabites who had been encamped around the city. Every available Israelite warrior then rushed out after them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ozias sent word to all parts of Israel, and the entire nation joined in the pursuit. The Israelites slaughtered the fleeing Assyrians all the way past Damascus. Without a leader, the Assyrian army was easily massacred as they retreated in chaos.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Israelites returned to the camp and were enriched by the immense amount of plunder and spoils left behind.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They took possession of everything in the surrounding villages and cities, finding a great abundance of goods.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: High priest Joachim and the senate (the principal men and priests) came from Jerusalem to see the great work God had done for Israel and to honor Judith. Joachim, who may have been the same as Eliacim, was a leading figure in both spiritual and temporal affairs during this time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They entered Judith's house and praised her as the 'joy of Israel' and the 'boast of our race,' acknowledging that God was pleased with what she had accomplished by her own hand. Judith is often seen as a prophetic figure of the Blessed Virgin Mary.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith was highly admired for remaining a chaste widow instead of seeking a second marriage. While marriage was highly valued in Jewish culture, her dedication to her state was seen as a special mark of virtue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It took thirty (or several) days to gather and divide the immense spoils from the Assyrian camp according to the law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith led the entire nation in a song of praise. The people joined her, either repeating the verses or singing along in a joyful chorus, much like the victory songs of Miriam and Deborah.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She praised God as the one who brings an end to wars and destroys armies. This passage is recognized in various traditions as part of the inspired scripture.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She described how the Assyrians had marched through the rugged mountains and deserts of the north to attack Palestine. They had boasted of their power to dry up rivers and seize the country’s springs to leave it in distress.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She recalled how they had threatened to destroy the innocent and treat the young as plunder.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: But the Lord brought all their projects to nothing, striking them down by the hand of a woman.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Assyrian leader was not defeated by great giants or Titans (legendary powerful figures), but by the courageous act of a lone woman. The text uses terms familiar to the time to emphasize the scale of the victory over a seemingly unstoppable force.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His defeat served to exalt those in Israel who had suffered under his hand.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She highlighted how she used her appearance—putting aside her mourning clothes and dressing in fine linen and ornaments—to 'deceive' the enemy for the sake of her people’s deliverance. Her beauty was the instrument of his downfall.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Her ornamental sandals and the sword she used to strike him are mentioned as the specific markers of his end. In ancient times, the provision of fine sandals was a luxury reserved for royalty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the Medes and Persians were astonished by her act.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith contrasts the howling and fear of the once-mighty Assyrians with the joy of those they had oppressed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who were once so proud were forced to sue for pardon as they were overtaken by the forces of the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She promised to sing a new hymn to the Lord, acknowledging His greatness and power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She praised God as the Creator, whose Spirit brought all things into existence at His command.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All nature—the mountains and the seas—trembles before His presence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith emphasizes that no physical sacrifice can please God without inner holiness. External rituals are rejected as worthless if they are not accompanied by a sincere fear and love of the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God will surely address the actions of those who oppose His will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The text warns of the eternal consequences for those who persecute God's people, describing a state of unending torment. This refers to the final judgment where the impenitent will face the reality of their rejection of God. These 'worms and fire' represent a loss of the vision of God that never ends.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After their victory, the people went to Jerusalem to offer sacrifices and free gifts. They first underwent ritual purification, as they had been in contact with death and the plunder of their enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judith dedicated the items she had taken from Holofernes as a permanent monument to God's victory. This act, sometimes called a 'monument of oblivion,' was meant to ensure that the memory of God's great benefit would never be forgotten.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The celebration continued for three months (or a full month), and Judith remained with the people in Jerusalem during this time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Afterward, she returned to her own possessions in Bethulia and remained highly respected throughout the land.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Entertain just sentiments of the Deity, and avoid all duplicity. This truth is placed in the strongest light in the first chapters of the Book of Wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
