text: He shows that their wisdom is all folly, and that they cannot withstand his power. There are mortal sins of thought.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Soul and body are intimately connected, so that the actions of one defile the other, and banish wisdom. Wisdom cannot remain with those who are enslaved to sin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The instructive light of the Holy Ghost will not remain with those who dwell in iniquity. Those who deny God or His Providence are eventually abandoned; their iniquity soon betrays itself.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Spirit having made and filled all things, must be perfectly acquainted with every transaction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is strong and jealous. He examines even the smallest deviation from the paths of rectitude.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Seek not with such eagerness your own ruin through an evil life. Do not throw the blame on death, as if you were necessitated to sin. God created man to be immortal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God created everything for health and good. Poison and wild beasts become noxious to man only after sin. It is this which infects the veins. All may derive an antidote from Jesus Christ.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wicked strive to draw upon themselves the 'second death' by refusing to repent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author describes the sentiments of epicureans who believe life has no remedy against the grave. They reject as fabulous accounts of miracles and suppose that there is neither reward nor punishment after death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Epicureans imagined that life began by chance and vanishes like smoke. They see speech and reason as merely a 'spark' coming from the fire of the soul, which they believe is mortal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The epicureans were convinced of the fragility of nature, but they drew false inferences, pretending we should only enjoy ourselves now. This they ought to have proved.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: From the disbelief of future rewards and punishments proceeds the epicure's life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Youth is the spring of life. The voluptuous conclude that we should make use of transitory things, but it would be more rational to despise them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They live by strange maxims—that might makes right—which few will dare to proclaim even if they act according to them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Infidels are not content to live in riot; they also persecute the just. This passage points out the conduct of the Jews towards our Saviour in a striking manner.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The prophets spoke to sinners in the name of God, and many lost their lives for it. Christ appeared to promote their welfare, yet this only irritated them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wicked judge the just by their own standards, ironically suggesting that if the just had God's protection, they would have been saved from their trials.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The disbelief of mysteries leads to a dissolute life and to the persecution of the just. The pagans failed to see the advantages of suffering.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lucifer was guilty of envy, striving to be like the Most High and bringing sin and death to humanity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: During life, the just are protected by God, and still more in death. This passage is very applicable to martyrs: temporal death is for them the road to happiness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wicked err when they suppose that the just shall be no more after death. If the hopes of the pious were confined to this world, they would be most miserable.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The sufferings which the just have voluntarily endured are like a refined sacrifice pleasing to God. At the time of their 'visitation,' they shall shine among the saints.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All the just shall approve of God's condemning the wicked. They shall be invested with a spiritual power that will appear most terrible at the last day.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Charity secures both faith and hope, which without it are unavailing. Those who have the virtue of hope will await the completion of God's promises.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Choosing a dissolute life is foolish, and the family of the wicked often imitate their conduct and become their torment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jews deemed being barren a reproach, but it is better than children born of a crime. Good works serve instead of children for one's eternal glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those born of unfaithfulness will be wholly destroyed. While the crime of parents is not directly imputed to the offspring, experience shows the fruits of such acts are often miserable.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: At the time of trial, God knows the works of all. It can happen that the children of saints are cast away if they choose a wicked path.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The offspring of the chaste and faithful is happy and honorable. Purity of faith is the foundation of all virtues pleasing to God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Virtue extorts the esteem even of worldlings. Many seek to win the conflict for the cause of continence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The plantations of the unchaste will not prosper, whereas virtue leaves an eternal impact.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Whether children of unfaithfulness live or die, they can be a reproach to their parents, reminding them of their lack of virtue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If a just man dies early, he is still in a state of rest. Whenever death comes, it is for his advantage; an immaculate life is preferred over the long old age of the wicked.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God sometimes takes the just early to protect them from the bewitching corruption of the world. Bad conversations can corrupt the best manners, but God is able to preserve the just.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The pleasures and goods of the world are like a fascination or delusion. Worldlings mistakenly believe these transitory objects are real and lasting goods.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: At the judgment, the damned shall have no excuse; their own conscience will serve as a thousand witnesses against them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Conscience will condemn or acquit those who have no other law, acting as an internal witness of their lives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: At the day of judgment, the just and the reprobate will see each other's condition. The wicked will realize they plundered the just and frustrated their holy labors in vain.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wicked will repent in despair, realizing they were wrong to hold the righteous in such reproach and ridicule.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They will be forced to admit that the just were truly counted among the children of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They will realize they lacked true understanding and justice, wandering far from the light of truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Worldly fame passes as quickly as a post or a messenger. Many whose actions are admired in history were actually the scourges of mankind.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Man's life passes quickly, and few leave behind a truly good reputation that lasts in the light of eternity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Such are the sentiments of the doomed, realizing too late that they focused on transitory things. The past time is used here to show the certainty of these events.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Life passes as quickly as dust or the tender wool blown in the wind.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The care for the just is with God; He provides for them and perfectly understands every transaction of their lives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The elect will receive a crown of glory at the foot of God's throne, finding every advantage in Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God will take up zeal as His complete armour, making the misery of the damned unchangeable and defending His own.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Every element of nature will become a weapon against the unwise. As we have sinned in all, we are wounded by every thing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Lord's judgments will strike directly and precisely to the mark.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He alludes to the power of God over all nature, using comparisons like military engines that throw stones to show His irresistible force.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is more valuable than physical strength. Those who seek her must remain connected to her maxims.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author speaks with the voice of Solomon to give these truths weight, acknowledging that all true wisdom and inspiration come from God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All power is from God and must be respected. Rulers must render an account to the common Master for how they used their authority.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God severely chastised even great leaders like David and Moses, showing that He expects much from those in rule.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those in power have a higher duty to perform. Ignorance is no excuse for a ruler failing in their responsibility.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God punishes or rewards all in proportion to their exigencies and the reality of their actions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The mighty must be warned not to forget God and themselves, for each must render an account of their charge.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This message is directed to kings and tyrants—those who hold high office among the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who judge others must remain holy themselves. Ultimately, everyone must answer to the sovereign Judge.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is easily found by those who seek her with the assistance of divine grace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is ready to introduce herself, standing 'at the door' of anyone who desires her guidance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Entertain a sincere and eager desire to be guided by the maxims of wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Divine Providence is always on the watch to facilitate our just designs and save us.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom begins with a sincere desire for instruction and a willingness to submit to correction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is the path that leads eventually to an eternal throne and an everlasting kingdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While ancient philosophers often claimed the invention of wisdom, true wisdom proceeds from the bosom of God and has been practiced since the beginning of time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom should be shared and diffused among others, never hidden out of envy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Solomon acquired wisdom, showing that it is accessible to anyone who seeks it with a sincere heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Life is something of a stage where all are equal before entering it and after leaving it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Recognizing he had no natural advantage over others, Solomon prayed for the specific wisdom required to lead his people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He prioritized wisdom over physical kingdoms or wealth, recognizing it as the most valuable of all created and uncreated things.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He shared what he learned without selfishness, a practice that should be imitated by all who are learned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom leads a person closer to God. While the wise do not seek glory for themselves, it often follows them anyway.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Solomon expressed his thoughts with sincerity and captivation, avoiding the presumption that often plagues the powerful. His soul’s greatness was seen in both his words and his wise works.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom grants an understanding of the times—past, present, and future—and the changing seasons and cycles of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The spirit of wisdom is manifold and active, eloquent and subtle, capable of knowing and persuading. It is essentially the Holy Spirit working within the human understanding.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This spirit is one with the Son of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is the brightness of eternal glory, a concept St. Paul later applied to Jesus. It is communicated to prophets and saints in every age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God's goodness is seen in how He communicates His own nature through the generation of the Son and the Holy Spirit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No nation has been left without God's light. Even among the Gentiles, people like Job and certain philosophers recognized important truths about the Creator, even if they sometimes erred in their practice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unlike the sun which is followed by night, the light of divine wisdom never fails, even if human wisdom is subject to change.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Infinite wisdom is present everywhere and directs all things with ease.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom teaches us to refer all things back to God and to imitate His works, whereas mere philosophy often leads to empty speculation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is the ultimate teacher of virtue; any science that does not lead to a virtuous life is essentially empty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Solomon’s wisdom extended to rhetoric, the solving of riddles, and an understanding of natural wonders like eclipses. He could judge the future by understanding the patterns of the past.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Solomon’s fame remains as an inspiration to leaders, though his own life serves as a warning that one must remain faithful to the end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Goodness and courage are the two most essential qualities for any ruler.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person’s natural dispositions are gifts that are perfected through labor and divine grace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While people are born with varying capacities for learning, no one is 'more' or 'less' infected by original sin than another.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Self-control and virtue are gifts that ultimately come from God. Solomon’s prayer for wisdom included a request for every related virtue, including religion and chastity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His entire prayer is a beautiful expansion on his request for wisdom in 1 Kings, serving as a reminder to all in authority to love justice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Rulers should govern their people with the constant awareness that they are God's children.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Temple Solomon built was a permanent version of the tabernacle, representing the dwelling of God among His people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Uncreated wisdom is God Himself, while the wisdom given to people is His gift. Solomon recognized this majesty while preparing the earthly temple.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No one can govern as a representative of God without the guidance of His Spirit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Our first parents (Adam and Eve) eventually repented and were saved. Before sin, humanity’s dominion over the world was willing; afterward, it required effort and ingenuity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In contrast, there is no evidence that the unjust Cain ever repented.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God used Noah as a just man to preserve humanity during the flood. While he was a skilled mechanic, it was God who ultimately directed the wooden ark and ensured its safety amid the storms.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom chose and approved of Abraham, who remained faithful even when called to go against his natural tenderness as a father to offer his son Isaac. His obedience was his true act of compassion toward God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lot was the 'just man' delivered from the destruction of the five cities (Pentapolis), including Sodom and Gomorrah.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom guided Jacob and opened a vision of heaven and the ascending angels to him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Despite Laban’s attempts at deceit, Jacob grew wealthy through the favor of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom protected Jacob in his conflicts with Laban and Esau and stood with him in his symbolic struggle with the angel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Joseph was the just man who refused to sin when tempted and was preserved through both the pit his brothers threw him into and the prison where he was wrongly confined.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He eventually rose to high authority as a ruler under Pharaoh and was recognized as one who understood the will of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Moses was the servant of God used to deliver the people and was honored as one with whom God spoke directly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even those who felt speechless or kept in ignorance were given the ability to sound forth God's praise through miracles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Moses, the prophet, governed the people with the utmost prudence and ministry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom protected them from every enemy (like the Amelikites) who dared to attack.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God used similar things to both punish and favor. While the Egyptians were tormented by their own river turning to blood, the Israelites were refreshed with water miraculously brought from a rock in the desert.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Nile, which the Egyptians wrongly worshiped as a god, became the very source of their punishment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Israelites' benefit from the miraculous water was made even more profound by the contrast with the Egyptians' suffering.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians, who worshiped 'dumb beasts' like asps and frogs, were eventually punished through them (frogs, flies, and locusts).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If a person neglects to reform their character in this world, they must expect the consequences of their life in the next.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God created nothing evil; disorder is the work of humanity’s choices, not the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God calls all things into existence and establishes them in their proper place.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God loves the soul of man, and His incorruptible Spirit is a proof of His love and divinity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He refers to the Promised Land as a 'consecration'—a sacred place where His temple was to be established and humanity's redemption was to be worked out.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God even used small things like 'wasps' as auxiliaries to help the Israelites, illustrating His power to use any part of nature.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While all humans are by nature prone to error, the Canaanites had hardened themselves through custom and malice until their wickedness felt almost 'natural.'

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s anger is always accompanied by tranquility; He manages even justice with a level of mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Humanity should not question the Creator's design, for we know that under a just God, no one is miserable unless their choices have made them so.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some have misinterpreted texts about punishment, but the core truth is that God’s virtue is always consistent with His justice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Because God is a God of clemency, no one should ever despair of His mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The pagans who witnessed the destruction of their idols but still refused to serve the true God remained in a state of darkness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is a core foolishness of human nature to remain ignorant of God when His existence is so plainly visible in His works. 'He who IS' is the most proper name for the first cause of all things.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many in the ancient world worshiped the elements—fire, wind, air, stars, water, or the moon—under various names. These were some of the most ancient and general forms of idolatry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All of creation—the heavens and everything else—is a constant announcement of the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The error of idolatry lies in treating a material statue as the residence of a god. While some more learned people saw them as mere representations, many wrongly worshiped the works of human hands.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Statues were often painted in vermilion—a highly esteemed color—on festival days to give them a false sense of life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Binding statues with iron or gold was a sign of their fragility. In contrast, the sacred symbols of the faithful are recognized as reminders of the divine, not as the physical residence of the Deity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is common folly to risk one’s life at sea with more confidence in a piece of wood than in the Providence of the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It was God who taught the art of navigation to Noah, enabling him to build the ark that saved humanity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Long voyages were once seen as a great act of confidence in divine protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wood that saved Noah was a precursor to the redemption worked out on the cross.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The invention of idols led to 'spiritual fornication'—a corruption of manners where people justified their own sins by the examples of their false gods.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Truth exists before any falsehood. Idolatry was a later corruption of human history, and eventually, it will be fully overcome.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Idolatry often began privately, as families made images to honor their deceased loved ones. Over time, these memorial images became objects of public worship.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While the arts of sculpture and painting are not sinful, they were once restricted because people were so prone to use them for idolatry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The name of God—His glory—should never be given to anything else.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Idolatry led to horrific practices, including the sacrifice of children and hidden, impure rituals performed in darkness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Such practices often led to a form of madness or delusion. Those who do not believe in the true God can give no real security with an oath, as they have no respect for the sacred.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is the one who ultimately punishes perjury, as He hears even when an idol cannot.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Because of His mercy, God governs all things justly, including the punishment of those who transgress.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After the return from captivity, the Jews had a profound abhorrence for idolatry. The art of painting, which can be used to deceive the senses, was seen as a 'fruitless labor' when misused for false worship.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: At the end of life, those who trusted in material things will be abandoned by the very items they worshiped.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is the source of both life and the rational soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Worldlings wrongly believe that gain is the only meaning of life. Our physical occupations are like the activities of children—transitory and eventually abandoned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In their pride, those who reject God often threaten more than they can ever perform and treat images as if they were living beings.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Worshiping beasts is particularly degrading, as it is a claim for oneself of the praise that belongs only to the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were famously punished by the very animals they worshiped—insects, frogs, and locusts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even when God provided quails to the Israelites in His anger, He still treated them as a father treats children, seeking their correction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were restrained from even necessary food by the very pests sent to plague them, while the Israelites were given quails as a new form of nourishment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'sign of salvation'—the brazen serpent—was an emblem of Christ. It had no power in itself but served as a token of God’s favor to those who obeyed His command.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The remedy provided by God was not naturally efficacious; its power came from His word. Every spiritual wound has its medicine in the Scriptures.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: During the plagues in Egypt, fire and hail mingled in a way that defied nature—the water seemed to nourish the flames as they destroyed the land.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were unable to banish the pests even with fire, forcing their own magicians to admit a miracle was at work.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God provided 'bread from heaven'—manna—which is poetically called the 'food of angels' to denote its excellence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This sustenance had its own substance but also seemed to adapt to the desire of the faithful, a concept that mirrors the spiritual experience of the Eucharist. Those who were unfaithful found it less satisfying, as they were focused on their own fancies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Humans are not supported by physical food alone, but by every word that comes from God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Favors must be sought with diligence; just as manna had to be gathered before the sun rose.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who are ungrateful or negligent will find their hopes frustrated, just as the wicked cannot expect salvation without a sincere change of character. Ingratitude stops the very fountain of grace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were justly punished with a horrible darkness, a reflection of the 'dark' and cruel policy they had pursued against the Hebrews.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They were imprisoned in this darkness like unrepentant slaves for three days—a prefiguration of the spiritual darkness of the Gentiles before the resurrection of Christ.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Their interior darkness was reflected in their exterior environment. They were filled with an extreme anxiety and seemed forgotten by Providence, as if buried in a dismal obscurity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the light from lightning provided no relief, as it only served to heighten their fear and confusion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptian magicians found themselves powerless, unable to secure themselves or imitate the divine miracle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians, who once worshiped and fed serpents as sacred animals, were now terrified by their hissing. In their distress, many seemed to wish for death, but it fled from them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A guilty conscience creates its own cowardice; while a crime might seem safe for a moment, it can never truly be 'secure.'

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Extreme despair often follows when a person gives way to these dark thoughts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Fear and the expectation of punishment fill the soul with a mortal anxiety.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The combined weight of terrifying visions and the remorse of conscience was overwhelming.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The darkness effectively acted as an iron chain, forcing everyone to remain where they were.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the most natural sounds, like the singing of birds, offered no comfort to those who were already terrified.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While the land of Goshen was preserved, the lamentations of the Egyptians were heard by the Hebrews. They are called 'saints' here because they were set apart as God's holy people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Conversely, God led His people in triumph. He provided them with a 'harmless sun'—a light that guided but did not molest them—and provided for them even in the desert.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While the Hebrews were originally without the written Law, it was preserved through divine guidance and eventual revelation. The Jews were selected to be the keepers of this treasure for all nations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Moses, the 'one child' preserved from the Egyptians' decree, was raised up to lead his people and eventually see the destruction of their enemies in the Red Sea.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God had promised the patriarchs that He would visit His people, and the night of their deliverance fulfilled these long-held hopes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Hebrews offered their paschal sacrifice in private, a bond of union between them and God. They resolved to remain faithful to one another, confident that the promises made to Abraham would soon be realized in Canaan.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'noble' first-born of Egypt—from the palace to the slave quarters—were slain as the final plague.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The description of the divine decree or the 'Word' of the Lord coming at midnight is magnificent. It prefigures the birth of Christ, who came into a world buried in the darkness of ignorance during a time of profound peace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Visions made it clear to the Egyptians that their miseries were not natural occurrences but a divine intervention.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This plague did not touch those who were protected by the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The high priest’s garments (his 'priestly robe') were filled with symbolic meaning. The four colors represented the elements, and the twelve stones represented the ancestors of Israel. On his forehead, he bore the inscription 'Holiness to the Lord.'

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: These sacred symbols and the power of prayer were enough to hold back the angel of death, showing that God did not intend to exterminate His own people along with Egypt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God foresaw the malice of the Egyptians, though He was not its author.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were in such 'solicitude' (haste) to be rid of the Hebrews that they wouldn't even allow them time to prepare food.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were blinded by their own obstinacy, eventually rushing headlong into the dry channel of the Red Sea.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: During the Exodus, the elements seemed to take on new forms—fire burned in water and the sea retired—all according to God’s will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Red Sea bed was made like a green field for the Hebrews to cross with ease. This miraculous change showed that even nature obeys the Creator's design.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as thunders had warned of the destruction of Sodom, nature itself heralded Egypt’s judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were punished specifically for their lack of hospitality toward the strangers (the Hebrews) whom they had received and then oppressed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While the Hebrews followed their own religion, they had acknowledged the civil rights and authority of the land until their deliverance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All the miraculous changes in nature maintained a divine 'harmony,' like the strings of a harp played with skill. These miracles do not break the laws of nature but show the Creator’s power to direct them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: For example, while the people passed through the water of the sea, the land of Egypt saw frogs entering its houses.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Egyptians were punished by creatures, while the Israelites were refreshed with 'immortal food' (manna). The book concludes by emphasizing God’s faithfulness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is synonymous with the Deity, the Son, and the gift given to men.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God's wisdom is eternal and unsearchable—beyond our ability to fully comprehend.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This wisdom was with God before the creation of the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wise will show their understanding by observing the commandments and the Scriptures.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is known and revealed through the Spirit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'fear of the Lord' is the beginning of wisdom, leading eventually to a crown of joy and eternal glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some are gifted with good dispositions from the womb, a grace that helps preserve them from evil. This mercy is intended for all of 'God's seed'—both men and women.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Knowledge should be applied with piety. A learned but impious person is a dangerous thing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: One must remain vigilant against sin and always be ready to repent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God's favor is evident in the lives of the patriarchs and all who serve Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Trials are a necessary part of life, but God will always grant sufficient grace to those who are humble.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Regulate your heart during trouble and remain patient. Do not wish for death out of despair, as that borders on an unhealthy longing to escape life’s challenges.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Patience is the expectation that relief will eventually come from God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Humiliations serve to show what a person’s character truly is.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who endure are eventually enlightened with joy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God rejects those who are inconstant or hypocritical—those who try to reconcile the service of the world with the service of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom seeks every virtue. Those who love God will bring forth the fruits of obedience.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God will eventually address any lack of respect shown toward parents.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even if you provide for your parents, you remain deeply in their debt for all they have given you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Honor both father and mother through your actions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Parental blessings provide a strong foundation for a house, while their 'curses' reflecting the consequences of neglect were anciently feared.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never boast of your talents or say anything that would disrespect those who raised you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: To be a father is a position of honor. Even the great Alexander was reminded that his greatness did not excuse a lack of respect for his earthly parents.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even as parents become infirm with age, they do not lose their character as fathers and mothers. Those who despise their parents cannot truly claim to serve God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Acts of alms and prayer for parents are rewarded. Kindness toward them is a source of spiritual increase.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Reviling one's father is a form of blasphemy against the Creator who gave you life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Meekness and goodness beget love. A person’s dignity should be the measure of their humility.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The more elevated a person is, the more they must guard against pride. True humility is taught by wisdom and is an essential duty for all.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The mysterious nature of God and His Providence cannot be fully understood; we must often confess our ignorance in the face of such greatness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Vain suspicion and pride can ruin a person’s judgment, depriving them of the 'light' of true understanding.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A 'hard' heart that fears neither God nor man eventually faces its own day of reckoning. Those who love danger will eventually be overtaken by it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An obdurate heart, like Pharaoh's, leads only to sorrow. Custom, when not resisted, can create a perceived 'necessity' for sin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The proud sinner will take no advice, further hardening their own path.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise man's desire is for an ear that truly hears and understands.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as water quenches fire, alms quench sin. We are not truly masters of what is superfluous but are called to help the indigent without delay.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not deal cruelly with the poor, for God will hear their cry and address any injury done to them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Respect for authority and magistrates is an essential part of a stable society.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those in office must be attentive to the needs and rights of everyone.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wise who serve others will be honored. While the Jewish priesthood was anciently tied to birth, the truly wise serve in a more glorious spiritual temple.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom often tests her favorites by leading them through a period of contradictions and afflictions—the noviceship of the children of God. The narrow path and 'crooked ways' of trial eventually lead to virtue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After the trial, Wisdom establishes a person and explains her leading.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Turning away from wisdom leads only to ruin and eternal misery.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Everything depends on acting in the proper season.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never be ashamed to speak the truth or ask for what is necessary to support life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There is a shame that leads to sin—like failing to reprove evil—and a shame that leads to grace, which involves a healthy abhorrence for what is truly shameful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not excuse the faults of others in a way that makes you a partaker in them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While ostentation is wrong, one must never hide the truth or fail to speak in its defense.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom and instruction are known through the words of the tongue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Always yield to the truth and never tell a lie.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Riches are called 'unjust' here because they often proceed from or lead to a lack of justice. They offer no protection in the face of death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even if you think no one can oppose you, remember that you must answer to God's judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not boast of your power, as the fall of prideful leaders shows how quickly they are punished.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is long-suffering, but He is a certain rewarder who will eventually address every transgression.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not take God’s goodness as an invitation to offend Him. Graces are offered to help us change, but a relapse into sin calls for great severity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s mercy is great, but His judgment is also certain for those who refuse to change.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The best time to repent is always today; we are promised pardon if we repent, but we are not promised a 'tomorrow.'

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Let your speech be consistent and your sentiments rooted in truth so that others can depend on you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be quick to hear and slow to answer. Constant virtue eventually leads to the promised peace of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person’s tongue can be the source of their honor or their ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Detraction and gossip separate friends and lead to infamy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Friendship requires consistency and making allowance for the faults of others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A lack of humility can lead a person to be humbled like the ancient king Nebuchadnezzar.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Affability and an eloquent tongue multiply good words and gain the affection of others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While we should seek peace with everyone, true counsellors—those capable of keeping secrets—are few.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A friend should be chosen with judgment and 'obtained through trial.' It is too late to test a friend's loyalty after you have already given them your confidence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True friendship is a perfect agreement of interests in both divine and human affairs. Such friendship must be founded on religion to be lasting.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Breaking a close alliance often produces the most bitter enmities, especially when one's imperfections are exposed to a former friend.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even in close friendship, there must be a mutual respect for one another.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While caution is often necessary, those who are true friends deserve an entire and unwavering confidence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A true friend is a defense for those they love, much like how Jonathan saved David.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A faithful friend is more necessary than fire or water. Friendship only truly subsists among the virtuous, for those who judge others by their own character are more easily imposed upon; therefore, we must always look to God for guidance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A godly person will instill sentiments of piety into their friends. As Pythagoras noted, friendship makes one person out of many.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A good education is like a seed that will eventually bring forth fruit, even if it is suppressed by the passions for a time. Wisdom should be the primary provision for one's journey from youth to old age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as heavy stones were used to try a person's physical strength, many won't even attempt the 'weight' of wisdom and piety because it requires effort.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While many prioritize academic learning, if it is devoid of charity and heart, it is ultimately empty. The unlearned who seek God will rise, while the learned without heart will fall.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom actually seeks out those who are looking for her. Self-control (continence) is one of her primary fruits.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Serving the Lord with fidelity creates a foundation for virtue that will never be overturned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While we expect wisdom from the elderly, it is not always present. As physical faculties decrease, the soul's wisdom should ideally increase.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Seeking the company of the wise is a great advantage; you can learn much even from a great person who does not speak.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avoid the company of the unrighteous, for evil communications corrupt good manners. The consequences of sin follow closely behind its commission.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who sow wickedness will eventually reap its fruits in abundance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ambition is the root of ruin for both individuals and entire empires.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not pretend to be more wise than you are, especially before a king. Rulers often dislike those whose superior talents might seem to eclipse their own, much like Saul's envy of David.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A position of judgment is exposed to many dangers and requires both prudence and firmness. No one should push themselves forward into such a role without being ready.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not allow your severity to be compromised by too much indulgence, nor become entangled with the sins of others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you have committed an error, do not despair. Pray with a sincere heart, avoiding both presumption and discouragement.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Show pity to others by reflecting on your own frailty, whether they have actually committed a fault or not.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never plot or machine against a brother; deceiving a fellow human being is always a moral failure.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lying is pernicious, and the idea that a lie is 'good' if told to prevent evil is a dangerous misconception.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In prayer and important speech, focus more on fervor of heart than on a long repetition of words. Aim for conciseness and sincerity rather than many words meant to inform God of what He already knows.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Respect manual labor and husbandry. Even the most famous ancient leaders and patriarchs followed such honest employments.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The consequences for the unrepentant are the eternal results of their choices. The 'worm and fire' represent both the remorse of conscience and the final judgment, which is more intense and lasting than we can conceive.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A virtuous person never approves of divorce except in cases of incorrigible behavior.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While marriage is a blessed state, some may be called to a dedicated life of virginity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Marrying someone whose character and actions are hateful is a recipe for an odious life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never forget the 'groanings' and sacrifices of your mother during her life and your own infancy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Honor the priests and give them the portions enjoined by the law, acknowledging their service with your own power and resources.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you are poor, God accepts even small offerings. Be sincere and personal when you confess your needs before Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Always remember to include the poor and those in service (like the Levites) in your generosity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not pick a fight with a powerful person, as they can sometimes 'turn the balance' against you through their influence. It is better to avoid all unnecessary lawsuits.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avoid contests with people who are easily enraged; such situations are full of danger.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The character and reputation of your family are a reflection of your own life. People often associate with those of similar dispositions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not mock a person who is currently in sin, especially when they are enraged, for that is not the proper time for correction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In public assemblies, while it is dangerous to oppose bold and popular speakers, it is sometimes necessary for the sake of the truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you take on the debt of a friend, consider it as if it were your own.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A husband should teach his wife to be chaste by his own example, avoiding the very traps he might set for others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Caution is needed regarding certain arts like music and dancing, which were once more licentious in their public forms.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While you cannot always avoid seeing enticing things, the greatest caution is needed to avoid 'gazing' that leads to misplaced desire.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avoid the parts of town where those of bad character are known to dwell.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Virtue is many times found behind a modest appearance. Dressing too much can be a mark of levity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The additions found in the Vulgate often clarify the original meaning and should not be dismissed as mere interpolations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not envy those who seem to prosper through unrighteousness. If you have faith and wisdom, you will recognize that such prosperity is like a summer flower that is quickly cut down.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The world itself will ultimately approve only of virtue. The wicked will find themselves condemned eventually by both God and humanity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The instruction and example of a ruler are very powerful. Rulers must govern themselves before they can effectively govern others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as a whole city can be infected by the vices of its leaders, it can also be corrected and inspired by their good and modest behavior.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Everyone desires to live under the rule of just and wise leaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is God who raises up rulers and establishes the nations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise scribe learned in the law must look to God for the light and success they need to serve.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never act out of revenge for an injury. The character of a truly great person is identified by a willingness to move past slights.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pride is hateful to both God and man because it invades the rights of the Creator and disturbs the peace of society. It is the root of injustice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Kingdoms are often changed because of the pride and luxury of their subjects and the negligence of their rulers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avarice and the desire for plunder are the common causes of revolutions. Covetousness is the root of many evils.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A prideful person has little compassion for others—or even for themselves—and their heart is often 'for sale' to the highest bidder.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pride is the beginning of all sin, as it involves an abandonment of God's law. Both Lucifer and Adam fell from their state because of this.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Because a prideful person abandons the law of the Creator, they easily fall into all types of iniquity and misery.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God frequently takes exemplary vengeance on the prideful to restore order.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God raises up the humble in the place of those who were high and mighty. Even powerful tyrants find it difficult to grow old with their power intact.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nations have repeatedly submitted to a new yoke when they have been led astray by pride.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God will even overturn the foundations of great cities when their pride becomes too great.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Conduct and character are far more important than birth. Wise men like Joseph and Daniel obtained authority because of their consistent behavior.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not let greed deprive you of what you need, and never do anything that is beneath your personal dignity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True merit is preferable to high birth because it has the power to overcome obstacles and lead a person to great heights.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not judge a person solely by their physical appearance. The robust are not always wise, and physical beauty fades with time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as the small bee produces the sweetness of honey, a person who is not physically striking may surpass others by their abilities and inner character.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: People have been raised from mean conditions to the highest thrones. Similarly, many powerful leaders have been forced to end their lives in total obscurity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: High birth is no protection from a fall if it is accompanied by a lack of character.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never judge a person or pass a sentence too hastily, even if it happens to be just. Great leaders have regretted the consequences of their snap decisions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Interrupting another's discourse is a mark of impertinence. Wait for others to finish and let those with more information speak first.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not meddle in too many affairs; it is the surest way to succeed in nothing and destroy your own peace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While some may have more challenging natural dispositions, God is not the author of iniquity. Every person has the power to choose their own path.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God never abandons a person first. All things work together for the good of those who remain faithful to Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Every person has a part in the divine covenant initiated by those who came before them, a role solidified through their religious commitments.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If God does not grant you wealth, consider that it may be for your own advantage and protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While prosperity was once seen as a sign of favor, we now recognize that being poor or simple can also be a state of great blessing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No one can be truly independent of God. While few would say they are as gods, many act as if they are the masters of their own destiny.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Christian hope is focused on the rewards of the next life, a comfort that the pagans lacked.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The last hour of life demonstrates the vanity of all worldly pleasures.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If children take an unrighteous path, it can stain a parent’s memory even if the parent was blameless. Real happiness can only be measured at the end of a life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not treat everyone with the same mark of intimate esteem. While alms should be given without too much inquiry, it would be wrong to encourage someone in their own ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: What you do for the poor is recognized as if it were done for the Lord Himself.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do good to everyone, but be especially supportive of those who are virtuous. Do not partake in the crimes of those who would misuse your gifts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Malice 'rusts' over time, eventually showing its true nature. Be wise as a serpent and remain cautious of those who have previously shown themselves to be untrustworthy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as a mirror must be constantly polished to keep its sheen, relationships with those who have betrayed you before require high vigilance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Caution is needed in dealing with anyone whose character is suspect. Do not familiarize yourself with what is dangerous.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The tears of the unrighteous are often like those of a crocodile—they cry while they are planning to do you harm.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pride is a natural tendency that is only increased by associating with the prideful. Keeping bad company is dangerous for both this life and the next.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An association between an iron kettle and a fragile pot will only result in the latter being broken. Be careful when associating with those whose rank or influence is much greater than your own.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Treat the promises of the powerful with caution.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be careful of those who use festivities as a way to exhaust you or obtain your services for their own gain.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not attempt to rival the sumptuous lives of the great, as it only results in your own humiliation and lack.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Depend on your own wisdom and character rather than the bounty of others. Be firm and steady in your principles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be cautious even in your most private moments. Disclosing your secrets to a great person can often result in a loss of their trust.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Similarity of character and condition is the best foundation for a true and lasting friendship.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be cautious in all your dealings, even in your speech and how you conduct yourself in public or at court.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Similarity in character and condition is the best foundation for a true and lasting friendship, for the good and the bad cannot easily find common ground.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Christ demonstrated His power by teaching people of vastly different temperaments to embrace a common faith.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is inhuman to push someone who is already falling.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A true friend is a 'helper' who prevents a person’s ruin and turns their words toward a good purpose. In contrast, the prideful are often fed by the applause of those who should have corrected them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who can control their tongue is near perfection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A miser is envious and refuses to use his wealth for himself or for the relief of the poor. The true use of riches is to take what is sufficient and give the rest to those in need.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who gathers wealth with too much greed often takes it from their own soul’s peace. Such misers are often derided and rarely change their ways before death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: By refusing himself the basic necessities of life, a miser rejects God’s gifts and creates a scandal for his neighbors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Covetousness is a 'wickedness' that often persists until the end of a person's life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Make a proper and holy use of whatever God has bestowed upon you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'covenant of hell' (the regions of death) is the common destiny for all physical life. We do not know the hour of our departure, and therefore we must be diligent in doing good while we can.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not let all your 'toils' and what you have amassed with care be for nothing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Use your resources for alms and lawful society with friends. Kindness to others is a holy way to use your time and wealth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Every generation fades and grows old like a garment, or like leaves on a tree. This is the natural cycle established from the beginning.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Only the fruits of the Spirit are truly immortal; everything else eventually comes to an end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who abide in wisdom have nothing to fear, as they are protected by the 'glory' of God’s presence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who resolve to be virtuous and keep the law will find that God's grace assists them in their pursuit of wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The love shared between those married in their youth is often the most durable and lasting.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise person will be heard with respect in both the church and any public assembly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Praise is unseemly in the mouth of a sinner. A person who teaches well but lives badly does an injury to the reputation of the truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom and success in speech are gifts from the Lord, provided in His own time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ignorance and vice are not the work of God but are the result of the perversion of human free will. God never ordains a person to fail or sin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Humanity has been given free will and the grace of God to support them. If a person chooses to yield to sin, they cannot blame their Creator, as the power to choose good was always available.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God would never impose precepts or commandments if human beings were not free to choose whether or not to follow them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Life and death, good and evil, are set before every person. Every individual has the freedom to reach out for what they desire.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not desire a multitude of children if they are to lead useless and unrighteous lives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Anything that springs from corruption will eventually end in ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who yield to the 'fire' of their own unbridled desires will eventually face the consequences of those choices.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The effrontery of those who commit sin openly and without shame is a mark of their own moral decay.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s judgment once fell even on a great multitude for their lack of faith in the wilderness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Every person will be rewarded or punished according to their actions. Even a small act of kindness—like giving a cup of cold water—will not go without its recompense.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s counsels are unsearchable, and His mercy is evident in all of His works.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is never indifferent to virtue; He created everything and no part of creation is beneath His notice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Neither God’s threats nor His promises will move a person whose heart is set on unrighteousness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Every person will face the judgment that God pronounces in His own time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The heavenly bodies and everything that subsists are guided by an intelligent Creator, refuting the idea that the world is a result of mere chance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All of nature obeys the Creator's orders. After setting the universe in order, God established the world as we know it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Humanity was designed for immortality and made to reflect the image of God. Sin is what caused human life to be shortened and corrupted.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Humanity’s dominion over the world has been greatly diminished since the fall.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unlike animals, humans have the understanding to know good from evil and the capacity to merit through their choices.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God has given every person a law in their heart and will eventually bring everyone to judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Observing God's law leads to life. This was the privilege of Israel, yet the natural law provided light to all nations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Gospel has perfected and fulfilled the ancient Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God's power was evident when He gave the Law on Sinai, accompanied by the 'voice' of thunder and spectacular signs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The second half of the Law focuses specifically on how we are to treat our neighbors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God watches over his people and has provided them with the light needed to overcome their natural tendency toward evil.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While God is the king of all the earth, He has a unique relationship with His people, acting as their guardian and providing them with instruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God does not allow His people to simply follow their own inclinations but provides correction when they go astray.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No injustice is concealed from God. Being good and understanding, He does not abandon His work but remains ready to spare those who return to Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Good works are stored in God's treasury. Alms-deeds and acts of kindness are guarded by Him with the utmost care.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Remove the occasions of sin from your life so that you may 'offend less' and remain on the path of virtue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Return to the Most High and turn away from injustice. Abhor what is abominable and join those who make a return of praise. One cannot merit perseverance, but must pray for it constantly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Join the 'side' of the holy ones who follow God and godliness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Since we all stand in need of pardon, we cannot expect absolute perfection from any mortal. Still, we must remains watchful and trust in God's great mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God alone is the Creator of all things. He steers the world with the 'palm of His hand,' and all of creation obeys His will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Man cannot add to God’s greatness. His works speak for themselves, even when human beings remain silent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is so incomprehensible that when a person has done everything they can to understand Him, they have only just begun. It is best to adore Him in silence and humility.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No human can do anything truly for or against God; His perfections are beyond our ability to increase or decrease.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even a long life of 100 years is nothing when compared to eternity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Our human misery can always call forth God’s pity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s mercy toward humanity is infinite and disinterested. He extends it to us without expecting anything in return.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s judgments are executed in His own time for His own purposes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The manner of giving is what often enhances the value of the gift. A kind look and a good willingness can surpass the physical gift itself.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is better to use medicine for the soul before a problem becomes severe. Mercy and repentance are available to us in the 'hour of visitation.'

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If we take precautions to avoid physical sickness, why should we neglect the health and good conduct of our souls?

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Admirable maxims from the Gospel—like 'pray always'—remind us that we should never be hindered from fulfilling our commitments to God. Wait not until death to be justified.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We cannot expect God to hear our prayers if we do not listen to them ourselves. Approach His majesty with repentance and attention rather than presumption.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Remember the final judgment, when every person will face the Creator and hear His final word on their life's work.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Use your earthly riches to lay up a treasure in heaven, remaining humble in all your dealings.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All things in this world are subject to change. Reflect on how quickly the 'wheel of fortune' can turn, raising the humble and bringing down the proud.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be mindful of the reverses of life. A wise person is always on guard against sloth and is ready to repent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is a rare thing to recognize and give due praise to merit when you see it in another.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Aim for accuracy in your words and judgments, exercising restraint in your soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Recognition of the consequences of sin should be a primary motive for those who are currently slaves to their passions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avoid 'rejoicing in high living.' Quarrels and conflicts are almost inevitable in situations where people are overly focused on pleasure and consumption.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Small, repeated expenses can ruin an estate, just as social and personal neglect of 'small' sins can lead to dangerous consequences. The best way to avoid great failures is to guard against minor ones.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Indulgence in pleasure can separate a person from God and their religion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Certain forms of unbridled physical desire lead only to physical and spiritual decay.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not give your 'credit' or your confidence to those of bad character.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who resists improper pleasures and guards their tongue will live without much of the contention that plagues others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wise conduct and guarding your tongue lead to a life of less contention.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Disclose your faults only to God and His chosen ministers. In ordinary conversation, revealing your secrets or those of others can be dangerous and unhelpful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you speak badly of others or divulge secrets, you can expect eventually that others will distrust and even hate you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some people are so eager to divulge a secret that it feels as if the information is 'bursting' within them. This eagerness is a marks of a lack of discretion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many friendships are broken due to groundless surmises that could have been avoided with a timely and honest explanation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A foolish calumny or false report can destroy a relationship. It is the role of a true friend to both warn and be warned when necessary.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If a friend has offended you, try gentle means of correction first.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Love true piety and strive to comply with the leading of wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There is a true wisdom and a false, 'unjust' wisdom. Strive for the wisdom that is pure and rooted in high principles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Beware of those who 'dress in black' or put on a show of mourning while secretly harboring unrighteousness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A hypocrite often abstains from outward crimes only out of fear. True reason and piety should direct a person’s behavior.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While one must be careful not to judge solely on outward appearance, a person’s countenance can sometimes reflect their inner state.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There is a form of reproof that is unseemly and unhelpful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is good to hear a person’s apologies and excuses. Those who own up to their actions merit pardon, while those who take correction well are preserved from ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Concupiscence and desire must be restrained by the virtue of chastity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Hypocrites and pests of society are justly avoided and even hated for the damage they do.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The prosperity of the wicked should never be envied, for their loss is eventually certain.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Real worth is found in merit rather than just in titles. Many would be deemed 'worthy' of power only if they never actually attained and misused it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A miser may think they are saving money by buying poor merchandise, but they often end up paying 'sevenfold' in the end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Favors bestowed with an ill-nature lose their grace and value.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A flatterer who uses guile for the sake of gain expects far more in return than they ever give.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Making a display of one’s favors only serves to excite hatred in others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who refuses to permit their goods to be of service to anyone else is justly viewed with distrust.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A fool often boasts of what they claim to have, but their language reveals their true lack of character.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Riches are misplaced on a fool; they may as well be without anything for all the good their wealth does them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A 'slippery' path in a high place is equally destructive to anyone who walks it without care.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An impolite or importunate person lacks the 'grace' of good manners.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If a person has had the will to offend, they must repent even if they were unable to put their intentions into action.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Shame that prevents a person from asking for the basic necessities of life is a form of self-sabotage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: People are naturally and justly displeased when a promise is broken.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lying is a vice common to those who lack a sense of their own honor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A liar throws society into confusion and undermines their own honor and virtue. Once someone is known as a liar, they can no longer be trusted with anything.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Discourses on proverbs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The great and powerful will often find ways to protect their own, but an escape from the consequences of iniquity is never truly guaranteed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Favors given to the powerful can act as a 'bit in the mouth' that prevents necessary correction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Hiding one’s wisdom is like locking up grain during a time of scarcity. Wisdom is given to be used for the benefit of others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While a just person may occasionally fail, the wicked fall into a pattern of continual relapse.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as a serpent stings in secret, sin inveigles the soul before a person even realizes the danger.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The devil is like a lion that goes about seeking someone to devour, but he can only hurt those who willingly expose themselves to him and consent to sin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Only the 'Sovereign Physician' (God) can truly cure the deep wounds of the soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Riches can often make enemies where none existed before. In the same way, the prideful often see their 'house' made desolate because of their own insolence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is the sure protector of the poor and handles their cause with speed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A sinner who rejects all correction is difficult, if not impossible, to cure. Those who fear God will examine their own actions when warned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise person knows how to avoid the dangers of being overly talkative and knows when to remain reserved.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Building on injustice is like building in winter or piling stones over your own tomb. Reputation or wealth acquired through ill means will not last.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All worldly grandeur that is independent of God will eventually end in smoke.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The path of the wicked may seem broad and easy, but it lead only to ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Practice and experience make perfect. Just as a painter who practices his craft produces better work, those who practice what they know of virtue grow in it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom identifies and promotes the common good.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wisdom that comes from God is like a well of life that never fails.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Senseless talk is disliked by any edifying assembly; the words of the wise are heard with respect.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: For a fool, all of life is in a state of confusion, like a house that has fallen into ruins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A fool views instruction as a burden or a chain rather than a gift.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A smiling countenance is good, but loud and senseless laughter is a mark of folly and should be avoided.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise person does not meddle in the concerns of others and will not easily accept every invitation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Entering another’s home with impudence—'peeping through the window'—is a sign of a lack of character.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A lazy person (a 'sluggard') neglects their own welfare and that of others. Such neglect is despised in this world and punished in the next.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person without character is like a lump of dung—avoided and treated with disgust.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is better to have no children at all than to have those who lack character and reason.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise and economical wife is her husband's greatest inheritance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Speaking and acting without restraint is a sign of a bold and unrighteous heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Only the wise know how to correct others with advantage. Instruction given at the wrong time is as out of place as music in a time of mourning.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A fool is incapable of understanding instruction, but those with sense can be corrected even if they have strayed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Deriding what is holy or wise is a sign of an empty heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: To turn away from God and follow vanity is true folly. A life lived in such a way is worse than death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While set periods of mourning are normal for the dead, there should be a deeper and more constant concern for those who have lost all sense of religion and reason.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as gold is heavier than other metals, the burden of a fool's company is heavier than lead.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A well-founded heart is like wood built into the strong walls of a palace or temple—it is not easily shaken.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A strong heart is fixed and established.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A heart without fear of the Lord is easily shaken in the face of trial, even if it seems intrepid in its own pride.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pungent reprimands can sometimes restore a person's sense of reason.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While sudden wrath is a failure, deliberate provocations are harder to pardon. We may withdraw our confidence from a person, but we must never withdraw our charity or patience.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We cannot master our own tongues without divine assistance. We need the help of a Good Master to correct our eagerness in speaking.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The discipline of wisdom is needed to restrain the 'slips and offenses' committed by the tongue and lips.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pure and noble motives are required for any person to truly master themselves.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God permits us to face the consequences of our own desires in order to lead us to a state of repentance. Remove all sinful affections from me so I may serve you alone.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Foolish and unbridled desires are a mark of an uncultivated heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Rash and false oaths are strictly forbidden. Do not make a custom of naming the Most High in common and frivolous ways.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No form of swearing is entirely 'secure.' Free people should give their word with such integrity that an oath is not even needed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He who swears falsely despises God and will eventually face the consequences.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not use God’s name through levity or with no intention of following through on your word.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Blasphemy—attributing to God what does not belong to Him—is a crime equal to death in the spiritual sense.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May the merciful and true believers remain far from such wickedness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Shame that is laid aside is a path to any form of wickedness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
