text: Regard your parents with honor, especially if you yourself are raised to a high position. Your attitude toward them will be brought to judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never wish that you had not been born, for every life has its purpose in the Creator’s design.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who refuses to be admonished is in a dangerous state, for who else will warn them?

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Three types of people are particularly unrighteous: the passionately angry, the libertine, and the adulterer.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Any furious passion—whether anger, avarice, or lust—destroys a person’s peace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lust is like a fire that is never satiated; it brings shameful consequences to both body and soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person driven by passion is never delicate in their choices. Obstacles only serve to increase their irregular desires.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The adulterer wrongly fears only the 'eyes of men,' displaying a profound lack of true wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom personified refers all glory to God. The true religion of Israel is far superior to any pagan philosophy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom is found in the 'power' of the temple rather than in the schools of philosophy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom proceeds from the mouth of the Most High. It is the grace granted to the saints and the light that presides over all of creation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Like the pillar of cloud that guided Israel, wisdom stands near the throne of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom has 'penetrated' every part of the world and the hearts of all people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Israel was chosen gratuitously to be the keepers of this divine light.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God offers His grace to all but forces no one to accept it. True wisdom finds its 'tent' among those who welcome it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Effectual grace is found through the Church. Those who refuse to hear the truth of religion will not receive its full benefits.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom was engendered before all time. It directs the ministers of the holy and has been with God from the beginning.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God has established wisdom as his inheritance among His people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is exalted and firm, like the mountains of Zion or Hermon.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom flourishes in all places—from the desert to the seashore—bringing life wherever it goes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: People should delight to 'walk' in wisdom as they would walk under the shade of trees in a hot country.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is as precious as the finest myrrh that distills naturally from the bark of a tree.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The beauty and value of wisdom are compared to the most highly esteemed gifts of the ancient world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is both rich and pleasant, a place of honor for all who seek it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She is given to all her children who are chosen by Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Christ is the fulfillment of wisdom—the way, the truth, and the life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The memory and influence of wisdom are sweet.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unlike carnal delights which leave a person disgusted, the more grace a person receives from God, the more they thirst for His goodness. Experience of God never fatigues the soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who follow wisdom will find their labor is not in vain but will be directed into the paths of true life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The book of the covenant is the true inheritance of Jacob. To study the Scriptures and stick to the Lord is the most glorious of all occupations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He who loves has fulfilled the core of the law. The truth of God’s word is certain and sufficient.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Christ is the eternal King whose Gospel, like an overflowing river, has enriched the entire earth. David was only a figure of this eternal ruler.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lessons from wisdom are intended to make a deep and lasting impression on the soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An adulterer or a person devoid of sense is a fool, regardless of their age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wisdom is a possession more durable and precious than any other physical wealth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ten things lead to happiness, but the most important is the fear of the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: To see the fall of one's enemies is a blessing, provided it is for the glory of God rather than out of a spirit of revenge.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prudence and wisdom lead to a truly happy life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He who finds a true friend and speaks to attentive ears is blessed indeed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All happiness is grounded on the fear of God. While fear often begins the work of salvation, love is what eventually perfects it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Heresy is like a wicked woman who conceals her malice behind a show of virtue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No plague is as bitter as those that target the heart, and no wickedness is as great as a soul detached from the truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There is no revenge as bitter as that which is wished upon one by their enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as there is no poison as deadly as that of a serpent, there is no anger more destructive than that of a vengeful heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is better to dwell with a lion or a dragon than to live with a wicked and vengeful person. Historical examples show the evils occasioned by those who misused their influence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person's beauty is dimmed by their own passion; their face darkens like sackcloth when their anger bursts forth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A spouse who cannot avoid hearing of a partner's misconduct will find themselves falling into despair and surrounded by enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I could wish no greater evil to an enemy than to be paired with a wicked partner.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Uneasiness and a lack of peace deprive a person of much of life's comfort.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wickedness is a source of profound unrest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Four things are particularly painful: a jealous partner, an unruly city, false accusations, and a treacherous tongue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is a terrible thing when an entire city rises up against a man or when everyone is charged with a common crime.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A jealous companion is a source of constant grief.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Backbiting and a treacherous tongue are at the root of many societal and personal evils.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wicked companion is an 'unsettled yoke' that suffers no one to rest. Relationships require a proper fit to be peaceful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who remains in debauchery is a source of profound disgrace to their household.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Impudence is shown through the eyes—a mark of a heart that has lost its restraint.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Guard your eyes from gazing where they should not, for desire often manifests itself through the eyes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as a fence or a stake is needed to fix a tent, a lack of restraint will eventually result in the ruin of a person’s health and well-being.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A virtuous wife is a source of joy to her husband. True faith is the ground of all other virtues.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A good wife is above all price and cannot be exchanged for any other wealth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who adheres to virtue is a 'holy' thing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A virtuous lifestyle is like the golden items placed in the sanctuary—precious and set apart for a sacred purpose.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A consistent and solid character is a firm foundation for life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is as tragic to see a veteran in poverty as it is to see a wise person treated with contempt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who knows the law but turns away from it to sin is less excusable than the ignorant.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those in retail are greatly exposed to the danger of sin. Eager pursuit of worldly gain can cause a person to forget their greater duties.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Involuntary poverty is miserable, but an indifferent or dishonest attitude toward money leads only to mutually beneficial cheating.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A marketplace can easily become a place of mutual cheating where sellers praise poor goods and buyers disparage valuable ones.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The fear of the Lord preserves the soul in a state of grace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even after sin is removed, certain defects may remain in a person’s thoughts. Dwelling on these can lead to perplexity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person’s speech is the test of their true character. To judge someone's disposition, you must first hear them speak.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as the fruit shows how well a tree has been tended, a person’s speech reveals the state of their heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A sincere desire for justice is the surest method for obtaining it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sin inevitably entails punishment; iniquity falls back on the one who commits it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The speech of a wise man is consistent, whereas a fool always mixes something improper into what they say. Virtue remains constant regardless of worldly appearance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wait for the proper opportunity to speak. Avoid the company of those whose discourse promotes iniquity, blasphemy, and conflict.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who fear God will turn away their ears from blasphemy and unholy speech.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Disclosing a secret to an enemy was anciently considered a crime of the highest order.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you betray a secret, you can expect that you will never be trusted again.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Betraying a friend is a grievous offense. The loss of a true friendship through betrayal is nearly impossible to repair.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While common insults can be pardoned, the revelation of a secret destroys all hope of future confidence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is difficult to guard against a false friend who acts pleased with you while actually seeking your ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Traitors and false friends often deal wounds in the dark, hurting others and eventually themselves.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He who sets a snare for another will eventually perish in it himself.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God eventually punishes the sinner, even when the person has forgotten their own offense.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Seeking revenge out of rancor is a grievous sin. We are called to never lay aside our love for one another.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Charity requires us to forgive those who are penitent. How can we expect pardon from God if we do not forgive our neighbor?

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is not bound to hear the prayers of one who refuses to forgive others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Remember the reality of death and your own corruption, and let that lead you to abide by the commandments. Do not rage against your neighbor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Most quarrels proceed from a simple misunderstanding or from ignorance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The more 'fuel'—anger or insults—you add to a fire, the greater the blaze will be.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Angry words are like fuel for a fire.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A 'third person' who carries stories between friends is a source of great discord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Indiscreet reports cause unnecessary dissension and should be avoided.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Gossip and detraction can even cause husbands to suspect their virtuous wives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Detraction separates the closest of friends.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The grave is a better place than the life of those who are consumed by unholy desire.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The virtue of the pious will eventually shine through any calumny directed at them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lending to a neighbor in their necessity is a work of mercy and a form of alms. Justice requires that what is borrowed be restored.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Failing to pay back a debt is what causes many to be unwilling to lend in the first place.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Acts of respect—like kissing hands or faces—should be done with sincerity rather than as a mark of servitude.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who makes a false promise of return is a deceiver.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Through the injuries of unpaid debts, many lose both their money and their friends.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not let the fraud of others deter you from assisting the truly poor. Lending is for the sake of justice and mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Follow the commandment to assist the poor, or else your own merit is lost.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A neighbor is entitled to your assistance when they are in need. Help them, and God will reward you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The poor are God's 'store-houses.' Those who are liberal in their help have many to intercede for them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not engage your word for more than you can pay, nor encourage the negligence of those who owe you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A surety is essentially answering for another person’s very existence or subsistence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An ungrateful person will abandon someone who has risked themselves for them. Everyone hates the ungrateful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not make rash engagements that could ruin your own prosperous estate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ambition and trying to take on too much can lead to misery.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prudence dictates that we should not ruin ourselves even when we are trying to help others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person stands in need of very little. Those who are content with the basics will not need to live as a stranger in another’s home.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The welfare of a country depends on the good education of its children. Correction and instruction are needed, always used with proper tenderness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A father sees himself reborn in his child. A well-educated child is a source of joy to all.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A parent may expose himself to 'wounds' for a child’s welfare. If he neglects correction, he will eventually bewail the consequences of his child's imprudence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An impetuous youth must be properly 'broken in,' just as a spirited horse becomes a great asset when properly trained.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A parent should smile but also be respected. Do not laugh at what should be corrected.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Youth is often destitute of experience and overly confident. A proper education is needed to steer them away from folly and towards wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Neglecting a child's education is a mark of failure in a parent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Health is always better than riches.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A well-tutored soul and a good constitution are preferable to all the gold in the world. Justice is the greatest of all advantages.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Rest in the grave is better than a miserable life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Food and riches are useless to the dead, and physical meat is useless to those who are too sick to enjoy it. Acts for the dead should focus on gifts for the poor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sickness makes even the most delicious meat useless to a person.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Grief and sadness for temporal things should be avoided by trusting in Providence. Only 'grief for sin' is of real service to the soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A joyful heart is the source of a person's life and health.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Love your own soul and comfort your heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Envy and anger shorten a person’s life. Too much sadness leads to sickness and can drive a person into a state of despair.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The study of virtue and the Scriptures is a help to remove temptations and promote honesty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Excessive anxiety will drive away sleep. A healthy amount of sleep is an alleviation for any illness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Not everyone has equal success; prosperity is often a result of God’s blessing rather than mere effort.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who is overly focused on getting rich is exposed to many temptations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avarice is a form of idolatry that leads to woe for those who pursue it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pride is the 'worm of riches.' It is a miracle for a person to remain humble in the midst of great wealth. While humanity has free will, those are truly happy who, by God’s grace, do not abuse it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God will bless the resources of those whose wisdom and generosity are recognized by others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Show not too much greediness when presented with a sumptuous meal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A jealous or avaricious person is like one who begrudges others' enjoyment, much like Judas blamed the 'profusion' of Mary Magdalene.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: When dining with others, do not reach out too greedily. Let the master of the house or the guest of honor be served first.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Use moderation in all things and eat in a civilized manner rather than like an animal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be the first to leave a meal rather than being the last person at the table, showing you are not governed by gluttony.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Politeness is grounded in virtue and consideration for others.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Eating too much is unwholesome for any person.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An insatiable person will find that their lack of moderation prevents they from getting a good night’s sleep.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While intemperance is never approved, we must recognize that a stomach may sometimes be overcharged. In such cases, take necessary steps to prevent a long-term disorder.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Idleness and intemperance are primary sources of illness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Just as physical bread supports the body, instruction supports the soul. A miser who refuses to share is held in contempt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not 'challenge' others to displays of intemperance like drinking contests.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A true friend warns you of a disorder in your conduct before it becomes a public scandal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person in power must exercise humility. It is most difficult to avoid the traps of vain honors when they are being enjoyed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Have a disposition of joy for the company you are in.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Music has long been seen as a necessary part of a meaningful meal. A lack of appreciation for such things was once seen as a sign of being unlearned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Music and conversation should both be well-timed. Do not pour out wise sayings when they are out of season.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Faithful people direct their songs and hymns to God, even after their meals are concluded.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you are young, speak only when asked, and even then, be concise. Sum up your speech and remain knowingly silent when appropriate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be content to be an equal in conversation rather than dominating it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Young people should leave the table for recreation once the meal is finished.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Always bless the Lord for His goodness and for His gifts. Failing to do so betrays a want of faith and gratitude.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who follow the light will advance in virtue and glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An unrighteous person will bend the law to suit their own desires rather than seeking the truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do nothing without seeking proper counsel. Prudent actions should be executed without regret, as God wants us to ask for advice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God can turn even the most challenging circumstances to the advantage of those who are faithful to Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prepare what you have to say before you speak to be heard with respect. Answer out of a well-tutored heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A heart without grace is like a cart wheel—constantly turning from one error to another without stability.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An unmanageable person often seeks to ridicule everything rather than seeking the truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The seasons andstations of life are by God's appointment. Some are dedicated to high offices while others are chosen for common labor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The sun distinguishes the seasons and the cycle of our holidays.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While all people are equal by nature, God raises some to high positions and leaves others in obscurity according to His wise will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God disposes of nations and their conditions with sovereign power and justice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All humanity depends on God's benefits. His judgments on each person's station in life are just and final.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Diversity of human conditions, and even the presence of the wicked, can be made subservient to God's ultimate glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wise collections of maxims help to guide the soul throughout history.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be firm and inflexible in maintaining your own personal integrity. Do not let others have unholy power over you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be the master of your own house and do not part with your rightful authority.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Every person and creature requires what is appropriate for their station, including both work and sustenance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A 'stiff neck' needs correction and discipline to be made useful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Treat those who serve you as you would want your superiors to treat you. Similar misfortunes could easily have befallen anyone.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not place your dependence on the interpretation of common dreams, for most are vain. True messages from God are rare.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Looking for truth in a common dream is as useless as trying to catch a shadow or chasing the wind.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While humans cannot confer grace themselves, God uses His ministers to perform His work in the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Divinations, auguries, and common dreams are all vain.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Most dreams cannot be explained and lead to confusion. Distinguishing a high message from the 'travail' of the mind is difficult.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not yield to the weakness of seeking magic or superstition through dreams.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Law forbids placing undue attention on dreams and superstition. Stick to the word of God, which never perishes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Experience gives weight and depth to instructions. A person who has seen the world often knows more than what is found in books alone.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Subtle understanding comes from experience and being widely traveled.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom often involves knowing more than one can immediately announce.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Great experience gives weight to instructions and can save a person from common errors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who fear the Lord will live a life of meaning and fruitfulness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is the protection that prevents a soul from falling or helps it to rise.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God abhors unjust gifts and sacrifices offered by those who harbor ill-will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God will not regard the gifts of the wicked as an excuse for their unpunished offenses.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Withholding fair wages is a form of shedding blood and destroying a neighbor’s livelihood.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Observing justice and virtue is the most acceptable sacrifice. External ones are secondary to the sacrifice of penance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Oblations of praise and sincere hearts are even more perfect than the ancient sacrificial laws.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A sacrifice of praise was the true spirit behind the ancient sacrificial laws.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God requires that everyone contributes according to their abilities. A poor person with a virtuous heart is most acceptable.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Give with a mind free from avarice and a sincere heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Set apart your offerings for the service of God. He will reward your generosity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not attempt to bribe God with ill-acquired gifts. Such things cannot buy an escape from justice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God remains the protector of the defenseless.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A model prayer for God’s mercy and for the conversion of all nations, offered in a time of great affliction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This prayer predicts a time when all nations will seek after the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May God be sanctified in the sight of the nations by His protection of His people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May the end of suffering come soon, for the malice of our enemies is at its height.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May those who have escaped the sword find a reason to enter into themselves and adore the true God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Let it be known that there is no other Lord, despite the claims of earthly monarchs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May God renew His wonders and show His power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Remember your people, whom you have called Your first-born.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May your sanctuary and the place where You rest be restored.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Fill your people with your predictions and favors as in ancient times.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A plea for the coming of the Messias and for the people to remain faithful to God’s promises.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May all people know that you are the God of all ages.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Discretion must be used when choosing words, companions, and a partner in life, just as there is a difference between kinds of meat.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Requite the words of others as they deserve, showing true discretion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Take into account a person's temper and character before entering into a close association.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Love is a violent passion, but nothing brings greater content than a virtuous and consoling partner.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Through harmony and concord, even small possessions can increase. Discord causes fortunes to be lost.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A companion and friend who turns against you is a source of great sorrow. False friendship is highly dangerous.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be cautious of those whose 'friendship' is only for their own benefit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Avoid the snare of associating closely with those who are constantly suspicious of you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A false friend is a stumble-block in one’s path.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not consult with those who are envious or jealous, for their counsel will be tainted by their own motives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not seek advice on kindness from the merciless, nor consult with the lazy about how to perform a work.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Seek counsel from those who fear God and are enlightened by His grace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A well-regulated heart is the most faithful guide. If we are not blinded by passion, our hearts will often give the best counsel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All truly good advice proceeds from the Lord. recourseto Him is essential while we use our own best efforts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Reason must lead every work, and counsel must precede every action. We must distrust our own limited viewpoint.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The heart gives birth to good or evil. Knowledge without charity is useless and can lead to the loss of your soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person who teaches wisdom but lives with a hateful spirit has no true wisdom at all. Their sophisms are quickly discovered.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person whose soul is replenished with true wisdom will communicate it freely and obtain an eternal reward.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wisest among us will eventually return to the earth, but the virtuous will be remembered forever.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not attempt things above your personal strength. Try only what agrees with your health and the well-being of your soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Personal tastes and dispositions are as varied as human faces. What suits one person may not be appropriate for another.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Honor and pay the physician, for his skill is a gift from God. The health of both body and soul must be regarded with care.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In ancient times, physicians were often kept at the expense of the state to ensure the health of the realm.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While some criticize physicians for their experiments, we must not condemn the prudent use of medicine. God remains the source of all true healing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God has provided nature with medicinal properties. Historically, even miracles like the healing of bitter waters showed the importance of using appropriate means for health.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The medicinal virtues found in nature were intended by God to be used by humanity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A skilled physician uses natural plants to create mixtures that bring health and peace to the earth. Powerful remedies can cause diseases to flee.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not place an unholy trust in physicians, but do not despise them either. To refuse the help provided through nature is to tempt God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All diseases are ultimately a consequence of original sin, and some may be specific punishments for transgressions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: If you fall ill, seek conversion and repentance as if life were ending, but do not despair, for healing is still possible.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May success in healing remain in the hands of those who offer aid.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sickness is a troublesome state and a reminder of human frailty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Show respect for the deceased through burial and prayer. Funeral honors are primarily for the consolation of survivors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Allow a proper time for mourning as nature dictates, but do not suffer grief to damage your own health or render you unfit for life's duties.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Excessive sorrow should be avoided as it renders a person unfit for any good work.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person's state of mind makes their circumstances seem sweet or bitter, regardless of their wealth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The chief value of attending a funeral is to be reminded of the end of all earthly things.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Let the dead be a reminder to you: their turn was yesterday, yours may be today.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Once a person is at rest, let your intense sorrow for them also come to an end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A scholar requires leisure to attain wisdom. Constant manual labor can be injurious to deep learning. Pastors should use their leisure for study and writing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Manual tasks, like engraving, require intense focus but do not allow for higher meditation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Metal workers perform their labor with great physical effort, which occupies their whole mind.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A potter must focus entirely on his craft or his work will be ruined.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The virtuous must find a balance between an active and a contemplative life. Ancient groups were famous for their dedication to such studies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: To meditate well, one needs knowledge, virtue, humility, and hope. Manual workers often abstain from these studies due to the demands of their trade.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wise and enlightened men were historically chosen for high offices like governor or general.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Traveling is essential for gaining experience. History records the actions of all kinds of people for our instruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Diligence and prayer are the primary means of acquiring true wisdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A wise person is often enlightened by the Lord during contemplation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Scriptures should be preserved with the utmost care throughout every generation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The memory of a wise man is more valuable than a thousand descendants.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Insights can be seen as a form of divine transport or inspiration of the spirit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All faithful children are exhorted to flourish with virtue and bring forth the fruits of a good life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Praise God with songs and hymns.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Use both voice and music to honor the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s power is evident in creation and in the history of His people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No human action can prevent the ultimate purpose of God's chosen ones.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The greatest miracles cost the Creator nothing to perform.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Providence will eventually show that all things were made for their proper use.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s ways are plain for the faithful, but stumbling-blocks for sinners. His justice turns fertile land into saltiness when necessary.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Before the Fall, all was happiness. Now, even the wicked still have a mixture of good available to them in this life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God provides the necessaries of life—like water, fire, and the grape—which the virtuous use for good and the wicked use to their ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The good or bad use of earthly things determines a person's ultimate fate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Angels and even fallen spirits fulfill God's justice. The latter are forced to obey, despite their bent toward evil.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is pleased with the execution of justice. The final ruin of the unrighteous will demonstrate His power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Human life is a time of trial and anxiety, a consequence of original sin. Eventually, we return to the earth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Whether rich or poor, all humans face the same physical end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A person without the knowledge of God is often disquieted by restless and frightful thoughts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There is no reason for fear in the heart of one who walks with the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Animals have an instinctual fear of death but lack human moral reason.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Death and destruction are the ultimate destiny for those who follow the path of the wicked.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who choose unrighteousness will find their names missing from the Book of Life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Grandeur independent of God ends like a clap of thunder. A corrupt judge may rejoice for a moment before his ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'unclean' possessions of the unjust will produce no lasting fruit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The wicked are like weeds that grow on a riverbank—they are soon gathered for fuel and forgotten.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A generous soul produces fruits similar to those found in paradise.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Discovering a treasure without labor is a blessing, but finding true wisdom is even greater.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Virtuous and noble partners in marriage ennoble each other.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Virtue gives more content to the soul than any physical pleasure gives to the body.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The sight of a virtuous person is as agreeable as the beauty of nature.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A husband and wife can best assist each other when they are united in character.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prudent counsel has allowed nations to succeed throughout history.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Piety gives more real courage than wealth, which often makes a person presumptuous.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The virtuous person is covered with lasting glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Do not live a life of begging through laziness. Everyone is bound to labor for what they need.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The life of a parasite who only lives off others is a miserable condition.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Death is terrible to those who live too comfortably in this world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The sentence of death has been pronounced on all humanity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is a consolation that we share the same fate as all who have come before us.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Submit to the natural cycle of life established by the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The question is not how long you lived, but how well. An evil life is what is ultimately condemned.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Children often adopt the manners of their parents. History shows generations can become increasingly wicked when they turn from the truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A parent who provides a poor example leads their children to spiritual ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It is a tragedy to lead others astray. A life that leads to destruction is worse than never having existed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who choose the path of the ungodly are essentially choosing their own destruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The death of a virtuous person is mourned, but the spiritual death of the wicked calls for even greater sorrow.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The desire for a good name and a lasting reputation is a proof of the soul's immortality.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be on your guard even in times of prosperity and peace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be ashamed of doing wrong, but never of doing what is right. Respect wise instructions even when you don't fully perceive their reason.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Follow what is laudable and do not be incredulous to all reason.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Your misconduct redounds to the dishonor of your parents, as if they had not raised you well.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Turning from the truth can cause even your own people to judge you.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Blush for lying and for any act that injures God's covenant. Avoid an idle or prideful posture during meals.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be ashamed of gossip and revealing secrets. Truthful berdiri firm, however, requires a 'holy impudence'.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Never be ashamed to follow God's covenant or to oppose an ungodly judgment. Pass sentence without respect of persons.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be scrupulous in following a friend's last will and testament.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be exact in business and do not fear advancing your fortune through lawful means.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Correct any fraud in buying or selling and ensure your exchanges are honest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Keep your possessions sealed and locked up to prevent the temptation of theft.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Keep proper accounts and records to maintain honesty among all.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Correct the mistakes of the ignorant and avoid unnecessary disputes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Parents should see their children settled at an appropriate time to avoid future difficulties.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A lack of children or a suspicion of misconduct leads only to fresh hardship and contempt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Negligence in raising children will be recognized by the community.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Guard the integrity of yourself and others from criminal affections.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Natural beauty can lead to ruin if it is not accompanied by virtue. Be cautious of what exposes you to temptation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Flattering familiarity is often more dangerous than the open churlishness of an enemy. The company of the unrighteous is always hazardous.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author concludes by praising God and the great people described in the Scriptures.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The entire world displays God’s glory and power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is sanctified in the sight of the faithful people of His heritage.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: From the works of nature, we can form an idea of God's greatness. He is the author of all nature and knows all things without having to study them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The world subsists through a balance of opposites, an order established by the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We cannot behold the wonders of creation without being filled with admiration for God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The sun is the most excellent of all physical creatures, providing light and heat for all parts of life. All creatures manifest God's majesty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The sun's motion is guided by the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The moon marks out the seasons and the cycle of time, acting as a guard for the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The lunar system was used to determine the timing of important festivals.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The name of the moon refers to its function in dividing and marking the passage of time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The stars are like a great army in the heights of heaven.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The stars and heavenly bodies obey God’s orders with perfect discipline.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The rainbow displays its various colors across the sky, a spectacular sight of beauty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God uses the elements, like storms and lightning, to execute His judgments.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the birds and the tempests are used for God's purposes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Storms can be as powerful as huge mountains.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The earth itself seems to tremble at the power of God's strike.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lightning and even swarms of locusts perform God's will in nature.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A heavy snow or shower can overwhelm the world; the brilliance of snow can even be blinding.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Frost and ice are like sharp stakes that cover the earth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ice covers the waters like a breastplate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Intense cold can destroy vegetation as effectively as fire.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Warmth and rain bring joy and restore beauty to the earth after winter.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is the Lord of the sea and has established islands within its depths.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who sail the seas see things that others can barely imagine.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The ocean is filled with whales and other monstrous creations of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God's providence provides for a prosperous journey, subject to His will.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Everything in the world and all that is within it obeys God's command.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: What is man compared to the Lord? We are nothing next to His power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He is above all praise. Exalt the Lord with all your power, for you can never reach the height of His greatness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author sets before us those who put virtue into practice; their praises are sounded in holy places.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God is the author of all virtue found in the fathers, and His glory is shown through their lives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Kings, judges, and prophets are praised for how they served God and instructed the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Rulers should be known for prudence and eloquence. Leaders instructed the people with wise words.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Hebrews were famous for their music and songs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many lived in states of power and peace, like Abraham.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Generations of the people have left their mark.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some have left behind a name and a lasting posterity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Others left no lasting memory, either through their own choices or the end of their line.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'men of mercy' are the true faithful who followed God's way.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people were always solicitous about being gathered to their fathers in peace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Henoch was a model of penance for all generations. Tradition holds that he was translated by God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Noah was found perfect and was used to save mankind.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Abraham is the father of a multitude and is the father of all who believe.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He was faithful to God, even in the readiness to sacrifice his own son.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God promised that all nations would be blessed through Abraham’s posterity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Moses governed as a father and was the mildest of men. He was honored more than any other prophet.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He saw God familiarly and faced down those who rebelled against Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He was shown God’s glory and overthrew powerful kings.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God gave Moses the meekness and qualifications needed to rule.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God made Moses hear His voice from the cloud.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Moses met God face to face and received the commandments of life as a remedy against ignorance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Aaron was the interpreter of the Law and was honored with the high priesthood.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Aaron was clothed in sacred robes and confirmed in his high dignity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His garments were elaborate, signaling his coming to all the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Craftsmen created these sacred items, including signs of truth.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The names of the twelve tribes were engraved on precious stones.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His attire was holy and spectacular, used on grand festivals.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The priests offered daily sacrifices for the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Aaron and his sons were consecrated for their sacred office.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The priesthood of Aaron prefigured that of Christ. A primary function was to bless the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The high priest and his brethren were the supreme interpreters of the Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who rebelled against God faced certain judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The priests were given their portion of the sacrifices according to the Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Josue was a saving figure for Israel and successor to Moses, endued with a prophetic spirit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He led the people and divided their inheritance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He gave signals in the heat of battle and conducted the wars of the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He successfully led the campaigns of his nation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: By his order, the sun stood still, showing God's power over nature.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God fought for him with the elements, outstripping the power of any sword.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Josue and Caleb were faithful and followed God when others rebelled in the wilderness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They acted with mercy and tried to prevent the murmurs that led to destruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Caleb was rewarded with land even in his old age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Judges of Israel were often saved by God's grace despite their failings.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May their names flourish again in the world to come.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Samuel served his people faithfully, even in difficult times.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God looked favorably upon Samuel and enabled him to restore the people's state.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Samuel was faithful in his role; his predictions were always verified.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He defeated adversaries and established peace during his government.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nathan the prophet maintained the succession after Samuel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: David was chosen as the best portion of the people for his role.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In his youth, he faced dangerous challenges with ease.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He liberated his nation from its powerful enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: David's victories sometimes excited envy, but God's plan could not be stopped.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God gave him power and glory. He defeated his adversaries decisively.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Psalms contain David's glory and gratitude; his pure heart pleased God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He introduced music and singing to the ceremonies of the altar.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: David adorned the sacred ceremonies so that people would serve the Lord with gladness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God remitted David's failings in consequence of his deep repentance and promised a lasting throne for his line.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The piety of David was rewarded with a wise son on the throne in perfect peace.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Solomon was wise in his youth, but later failed through unholy associations and idolatry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Solomon explored the secrets of nature and became famous throughout the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His wisdom caused people everywhere to praise the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He amassed vast wealth in great abundance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: However, Solomon yielded to unholy desires, allowing himself to be led into ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He defiled his reputation through unholy associations, though God's mercy preserved his line.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people became rebellious under his successor, leading to a revolt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Because of David's piety, the scepter was preserved for his family.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God fulfilled His promises to David by preserving a remnant to govern.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Elijah arose with a burning zeal for God, as a light for the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Through his zeal, he defeated false priests and foretold God's judgments.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He brought down fire from heaven to show God's power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Elijah (Elias) was a man of God who raised the son of the widow of Sarepta from the dead.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prophets perform what they foretell to show the certainty of events. Elijah warned unholy kings whose honors were later broken as a result of their sins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Elijah was reprimanded by the Lord when he betrayed fear, or as a sign of judgment regarding the state of Israel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He was sent to reward the faithful or punish the guilty. Elisha (Eliseus) was called from the plow to follow him and eventually lead the communities of the prophets.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tradition holds that Enoch and Elijah will return to convert many before Christ’s second coming. Elijah, who is still 'alive' in another state, serves as a sign of our future resurrection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who remain faithful to God can expect an immortal name when the great prophets return.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Elisha received a double portion of his master’s spirit and stood firm even before unholy princes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Elisha had miraculous knowledge of distant events and even raised the dead. Miracles through his relics showed he was a true prophet of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Hezekiah (Ezechias) fortified his city and secured its water supply.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: During Hezekiah's reign, the people were threatened with destruction by the king of Assyria.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God purified His people through the ministry of Isaiah. Prayer proved more effective than any troop of soldiers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: All of Isaiah’s predictions and visions were eventually verified.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Josiah (Josias) was a king of such integrity that the Scriptures reproach him with no fault. He was as pleasing to God as a masterfully crafted perfume.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Josiah began his reign as a child but eventually set to work with zeal to convert the people back to the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He boldly exerted himself in God’s service, even when iniquity was fashionable, and invited all the tribes to celebrate the Passover.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While some kings sought repentance, others tolerated unholy worship in 'high places' rather than destroying it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Later kings failed and lost their crown by turning away from righteousness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: One king requested Assyrian help, only to find his nation made tributary to them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jerusalem eventually became desolate, with its holy festivals unattended.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jeremiah foretold both the destruction and the eventual restoration of the kingdom.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ezekiel (Ezechiel) was shown the visions of God and the majesty of the Cherubim.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Ezekiel announced both the misery of the people and the future liberation of the penitent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The tombs of the prophets were anciently honored. Daniel is not mentioned here because he served primarily in the royal court.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Personal tokens of honor and influence were used as signs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Josedec was a spiritual figure who helped build the second temple after the exile.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nehemiah (Esdras) worked tirelessly for the people's welfare and the rebuilding of their homes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Enoch stood up for the Lord among a corrupt people and was taken by God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Simon 'the Just' was famous for his piety. He oversaw important repairs to the temple and protected the sanctuary from unholy entrance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He repaired the massive walls and buildings around the temple mountain.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He secured the city's resources by repairing its reservoirs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He saved his people during times of imminent danger.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: On the Day of Expiation, the high priest would appear from the 'house of the veil' to purify the people, surrounded by sacred ministers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The high priest's appearance in his majestic robes was as dazzling as the morning star or a bright cloud.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He brought a dazzling effulgence to the temple of the Most High.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Clad in sacred vestments, the high priest appeared with a beauty that denoted his great authority.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His attire of gold and jewels cast light all around as he offered peace-offerings before the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He appeared among the other priests as a tall palm tree among its young forest of shoots.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He presented unblemished victims with all due solemnity before the Almighty King.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He poured out the wine upon the fire according to the ancient laws.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The whole sacrifice was pleasing to the universal King.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sacrifices were offered as memorials of God’s covenant and sovereign dominion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Sweet and melodious sounds were made in the House of God during the ceremonies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While under foreign rule, the high priest and the people prayed with one accord for God’s protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God granted the high priest's prayer and protected the temple from those who attempted to enter the inmost recesses.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author exhorts the people to remain grateful and full of hope after seeing these wonders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May the name of the faithful be established for ever in God’s time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There was a holy indignation against those who sought to persecute God’s people. The 'foolish people' were those who mixed true worship with idolatry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who do not know the true way adore what they do not understand.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jesus bar Sirach concludes with a blessing of peace for all who observe his wise instructions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author lived through times of upheaval and travel, eventually retiring to Egypt. We must always show gratitude to God for favors we have not deserved.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: False accusations were made against me by those who sought my destruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I recount the dangers I faced—threats of torment and death—only to highlight God's saving power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God delivered me from the midst of a fire which I did not deserve, preserving my life through His mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I was preserved from death when my soul approached the grave because of unjust accusations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I remember the mercy God has shown throughout history to those delivered from their enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I raised my voice from the earth in supplication to the Lord.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I called upon the 'Father of my Lord,' a passage that clearly points to the belief in the Second Person of the Holy Trinity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even in my youth, I sought for wisdom through prayer and innocence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: My heart delighted in wisdom as it ripened like a grape. I sought this treasure until my end.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I found the benefit of instruction for myself.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Attaining wisdom requires wrestling against idleness and seeking God in earnest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: My understanding was purified through the practice of virtue.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True understanding is seated in the heart, and I sought it with all my soul.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: I sought wisdom with all my solicitude and have not been forsaken.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Lord has given me a 'tongue' of eloquence to praise Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Baruch (c. 600 B.C.) begins his account.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Baruch wrote this account in Babylon while the king was held in prison.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This was written in the fifth year after the city was compromised. At that time, priests and a temple still existed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people met to hear the words of the prophet and the contents of this letter.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The gathering was by the great river Euphrates.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Joakim oversaw the spiritual needs of the people during their captivity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: King Zedekiah (Sedecias) requested silver vessels to replace the golden ones that had been taken away.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The letter was read to all the people and the prisoners held in Babylon.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people requested offerings and oblations be made for them, along with petitions for the forgiveness of their sins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They prayed for the welfare and long life of their rulers out of a sense of gratitude and duty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They prayed that God would open their eyes to the reason for their captivity and cause them to detest their sins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They continued to observe the proper festival days prescribed by the Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The curses against transgressors in the Law were felt by everyone; the land was no longer flourishing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: To serve God is the same as to worship Him and sacrifice to Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After the death of Josiah, the nation fell into great misery. Its kings were imprisoned and everything changed quickly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people confess their transgressions. Some were reduced to such extremity that they suffered tragic consequences, as Jeremiah foretold.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We have become a derision to our neighbors and are ruled by foreign monarchs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We have been brought low rather than being raised up, a consequence of our disobedience.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God has 'watched' over us to punish us. Now He has acted in justice.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s laws are called 'justices' because observing them makes a person just.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: May God hear us from His holy House, whether on earth or in heaven.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those in the grave cannot glorify God's justice. The living must make His perfections known and appeal to His glory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A soul that humbles itself before God is most graciously received. Fasting and humiliation are required for true prayer.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We do not boast of any good works, trusting only in God's mercy and pity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: We were admonished to serve our rulers peacefully but chose to rebel instead.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: As a result, the land has become a desert and a former place of beauty is now desolate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Soldiers ransacked the tombs of the people, an event that was deeply afflicting to the Jews.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people have been driven into banishment and captivity as a consequence of their sins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Today, only a few are left to attend to sacred duties, for many of the temple's vessels were taken away.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people pray that they may be seriously converted in heart.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They confess they have been like cattle with a 'hard neck,' insensible to the correction they received.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Turn away from the path of error.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A new and everlasting covenant was promised through Christ. The Law of Moses was in force until the coming of the Savior.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Human life is very short; the people plead for mercy, asking if their failings are worthy of God's eternal wrath.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those in sin and misery seem near death, yet they may revive through grace and the memories of God's ancient promises.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people pray for salvation by God's power and for the sake of His own name.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God warns that disobedience leads to the 'debt' of being forced to borrow from others. In ancient times, this could lead to parents being forced to sell their children to pay for their debts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom is found in God alone and in those to whom He chooses to communicate it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people in captivity felt 'dead' or buried, living among unholy populations where they faced constant spiritual defilement.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Peace is found only by those who observe the Law; it does not exist for the wicked.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Few are truly wise, and many powerful people miss their aim by disregarding God's Law. Though Israel received the Law, its full fruit is yet to be realized.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Powerful nations may esteem themselves as lords of nature, but they remain subject to God, the Creator of all life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Will the unrighteous not eventually find the bitter fruit of their works?

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The heirs of conquerors are often just as much in the dark as those who came before them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom was not found in the capital of the Edomites nor among the industrious Phoenicians.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While some nations delighted in fables and wise sayings, true instructors should be praised rather than those who devise false gods for profit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The whole world belongs to God, yet few have the wisdom to recognize Him as its true Owner.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God did not prefer the giants of history over the faithful, such as Noah and the people of Israel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No human industry can procure wisdom; it is dispensed by God alone. His grace makes true discovery easy for those who seek Him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God’s wisdom continues while earthly kingdoms change and pass away.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nature itself trembles at God’s command; the sun and heavenly bodies obey His signal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The stars and heavenly bodies act as God’s faithful soldiers, watching over the world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: True wisdom and happiness for Israel are found in the Law of God. While the Law promised temporal blessings, the faithful receive an eternal reward.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Be faithful to your inheritance, for if you turn away, others—like the nations who embrace the Gospel—will take your place.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: You who are left must support and restore the nation. This part of the message is offered for your comfort.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people were sold into slavery like prisoners taken in war.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jerusalem is personified as a widow who must turn back to the Eternal Creator who nurtured her.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The gathering place of the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The foreign captors were described as a nation with a strange language sent against the disobedient.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jerusalem comforts her children with the assurance of God’s ultimate mercy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prosperity is set aside for the sackcloth of penance during times of public distress.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Predictions of restoration were more fully realized when all nations eventually heard the Gospel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Now that you have been converted, seek the Lord with ten times more zeal than before.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God has named you as His servant and His intimate friend.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Wretched are those who rejoiced in the fall of the holy city; they will face their own judgment.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Babylon itself was destined to fall to its conquerors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The ruins of fallen cities appear as if haunted by the consequences of war and unholy spirits.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people will eventually be gathered from every direction back to their own land.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Put on the spirit of justice and mercy, for God is bound to fulfill His promises.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Christ, the 'Sun of Justice,' effects the true restoration of His people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jerusalem is distinguished both after the captivity and even more when Christ gives peace to His Church.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Arise and remember God's Law with pleasure.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The return of the captives was a triumph, aided by the resources and convoys of those who favored them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Under God’s protection, the people walk diligently. The way is made easy for their return.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the desert sands prove to be as a delightful shade for those returning to God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A time of transition in history (approx. 336 B.C.).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A pivotal year in ancient history (approx. 323 B.C.).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The rise of a new era (approx. 175 B.C.).

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical events occurring around 170 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events of the year 170 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Times of upheaval around 168 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The desecration of the city in 167 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 167 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The account of Alexander the Great begins; he founded a great monarchy that conquered the known world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Alexander was famous for never being defeated in battle throughout his wide campaigns.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The world became quiet and obedient before him, from Greece to the furthest East.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prosperity caused him to seek divine honors as a 'king of the world'.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Alexander died after a period of sickness, proving that even one who claimed to be a god was but a man.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Before his death, he left his kingdom to the 'most worthy' among his followers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Alexander reigned for only twelve years, dying at about thirty-three years of age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After his death, his generals eventually divided the empire into four primary kingdoms.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Antiochus Epiphanes (the 'Illustrious') usurped the throne; despite his title of 'god appearing,' his character was wretched.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unrighteous men attempted to supplant the holy high priests and introduce pagan customs, falsely blaming religion for their misfortunes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They built places for pagan exercises and wrestling in Jerusalem.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: These renegades turned away from the holy covenant, becoming instruments for evil.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Antiochus coveted Egypt but turned back to attack the Jews upon hearing they had rejoiced at a false report of his death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He entered the sanctuary with pride and plundered the temple, actions condemned by both sacred and profane authors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His collector, Apollonius, basely attacked unsuspecting citizens on a Sabbath day.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He set the city on fire and destroyed a great part of it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy fortified the castle of Sion in the City of David to maintain a garrison there.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This site became a place of constant adversity, as if an evil spirit were watching to harm the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many fled to the mountains; groups like the Samaritans sought to flatter the king by adopting pagan names for their worship.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Antiochus decreed his kingdom should be 'one' in religion, attempting to root out the true faith and force the Greek way of life on all.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many yielded out of fear to the new worship and pagan services.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Officers forced the people to eat forbidden meats as a mark of their submission to the king.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An idol was set up on the holy altar, a figure of what would later be done by the Romans and what the Antichrist will attempt.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unholy symbols were placed even at the doors of common homes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The books of the Law and other sacred writings were torn up and burned; pious people hid what they could.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: People were compelled to sacrifice monthly on the anniversary of the idol's dedication.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pagan altars were set up over or against the true altar of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A time of great testing in 167 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events of 168 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mathathias, of a distinguished priestly family, retired to Modin to escape the impious orders. His family would eventually rule for over a century.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The name signifies a troop or one who is fortunate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A term meaning 'ardent' or a destroyer of evil.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas Machabeus, the 'Conqueror in God,' became the primary leader in holy warfare.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Names signifying 'undaunted' and 'abundant in counsel'.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mathathias lamented the ruin of the holy city and its sanctuary.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The family put on sackcloth to appease God's indignation through prayer and penance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Persecutors first tried to delude the family with flattery and promises of wealth if they would comply.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The king's agents claimed such actions were 'profitable,' though they were actually a dreadful crime.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mathathias declared that they would not depart from the Law in any direction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: When an apostate Jew came to sacrifice to the idol, Mathathias slew him. This was a legal execution of the Law against idolatry.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He called on everyone zealous for the Law and true justice to follow him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many fled to the desert but were slaughtered on the Sabbath because they believed it was unlawful to defend themselves on that day.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Their simplicity was pious, but their understanding of the Law was incomplete; the Church teaches that the Law must yield to necessity in emergencies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mathathias and his companions prudently determined they must defend themselves if attacked on the Sabbath.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Assideans, a group of pious men, joined the cause, zealous for the Law and proper worship.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They turned their efforts against the apostates who had joined the enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They successfully resisted the power of Antiochus and prevented the abolition of their religion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A virtuous person is one who is next to the King of Kings in status.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The priesthood was established to be everlasting throughout the duration of the ancient Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Joshua (Jesus Nave) and Caleb gave a true report of the land and followed God faithfully.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The virtue of mercy was characteristic of the leaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mathathias chose his sons for leadership: Judas as the commander and Simon as the wise counselor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mathathias died in the year 146 of the Grecian era, after serving as the head of his nation for about twelve months.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Chronicle of the year 3838 A.M.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 3839 / 165 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical events of the year 3839.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas Machabeus, the eldest son, was chosen to lead. His skill and valor entitled him to this post, and he was obeyed by all virtuous people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas gained four great victories against the generals of the unholy monarchs of the time.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Through his efforts, the enemy was forced to retire, clearing the way to purify the holy temple.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The term 'perishing' was used to describe those in a state of great misery.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Apollonius, the general who had previously pillaged Jerusalem, was defeated.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Renegade and wicked apostates were often the most virulent enemies of the faithful.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Bethoron was a location north of Jerusalem.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas prepared for battle through fasting and prayer. True confidence in God gives courage even when facing a strong enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy generals were so confident of success that they brought merchants to buy the Jews who would be taken as slaves.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those who plunder temples and seize religious revenues often find themselves in constant need. Sacrilege seldom leads to lasting prosperity for a monarch.

author: Haydock (Revised)
