text: While Antiochus showed vestiges of magnificence, his conduct was often viewed as abject and disgusting. He staged costly games even while his control was failing.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Internal revolts further weakened the king's power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the capture of rebellious leaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Former governors betrayed their posts to join the unholy cause.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Emmaus, west of Jerusalem, was the site of a major battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The chains the enemy brought for their expected prisoners were eventually turned against them by Judas.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Fasting and penance are the best armor for those fighting in a holy cause.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Since the people could not go to Jerusalem, they gathered at Mizpah (Maspha), an ancient site of national assembly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pagans attempted to defile the books of the Law with images of their idols, making them useless for worship. The sacred books served as a preservative of faith.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people rescued what ornaments they could, lamenting that they could not offer the proper sacrifices as prescribed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They sounded the trumpets as a sign of God's promised protection.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas strictly followed the Law, releasing those newly married or who had just built a house, even though his forces were few.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Following the Law inspired the remaining troops with courage and led to victory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events of 165 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 3839.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Context of the year 3840.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 3840.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Several apostate Jews served as guides for the enemy forces.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Vigilant leaders and pastors preserve the people from the stratagems of their enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Enemy forces suppressed the people while supposing Judas was engaged elsewhere.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas led a small force, many ill-armed through poverty, yet their confidence was in God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Priests in the army sounded the trumpet to signal the onset of battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy fled in all directions, some toward Philistia and others toward Idumea.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Great booty was captured, including gold and precious fabrics. The pursuit was paused for the Sabbath.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people sang hymns of praise and thanksgiving for their deliverance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Continuing the history of the rebellion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the destruction of the unrighteous.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas purified the sanctuary and chambers of the temple that had been burnt by the enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Guard were set to watch the enemy garrison while the work of purification was performed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Priests without blemish were chosen for the sacred work.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The idols and unholy statues set up in the temple were demolished and removed as unclean.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The true altar had been profaned by the erection of a pagan idol over it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people used unhewn stones to rebuild the altar as prescribed. Lacking a prophet for direction, they stored the old stones until one should arise.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unhewn stones were judged most appropriate for the new altar.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The inner structures and chambers of the temple were repaired.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: New curtains (veils) were made for the entrance of the Holy of Holies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Exactly three years after its profanation, the temple was purified and rededicated with great joy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The temple was adorned with golden shields; people carried branches to commemorate their time of suffering, a festival known as Hanukkah.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The festival of Hanukkah was decreed to be kept every year; the tradition of lighting lamps testifies to the joy of the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Fortifications were built to protect the land against the people of Idumea who had seized various cities.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 163 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Context of 163 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events of 163 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 3841.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Surrounding nations were always ready to oppress the Jews, serving as a recurring trial for God's people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the battle at the 'ascent of scorpions'.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Campaign against neighboring hostile groups.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy sometimes faced total destruction according to the vows of the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas defeated the enemy at Jazer and the surrounding villages.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Many towns had been seized by hostile neighbors, forcing the faithful to gather in fortresses for safety.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A hostile general named Timothy was defeated in battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the land of Tob.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Pagans in Galilee were encouraged to fall upon and destroy the Jews.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Simon Machabeus gathered the people in safe locations to protect them from persecution.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas marched with secrecy through the desert to surprise the enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Nabutheans remained friendly to the Jews during these campaigns.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas captured several fortresses, including those dedicated to unholy moon deities.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The capture of several key fortresses.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the capture of Bozra.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events of the battle at Raphon.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy general made decisions based on superstitious signs rather than reality.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Scribes in the army were powerful officers responsible for troop registers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas defeated Timothy and destroyed a temple dedicated to Astarte.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The city of Ephron was destroyed because it unjustly blocked the people's path.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Movements through the great plains.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A good leader stays with the most vulnerable part of the force during a retreat.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: None of Judas' men were lost in this particular stage of the campaign.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Leaders motivated by base motives who acted without proper authorization suffered defeat. God does not favor those who depend only on themselves.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Experienced enemy warriors retired to the city of Jamnia.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Those not properly called to the task of saving God's people find little success, whether in military or spiritual matters.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas and his brothers were recognized as those through whom salvation was given to Israel.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Hebron, which had been seized by enemies, was retaken.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Victories over the Philistine cities.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Priests who were overly eager for battle sometimes fell, showing that bravery must be joined with prudence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 164 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 3841.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical context of the year 3841.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events of 164 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Antiochus Epiphanes attempted to plunder a rich temple in Persia; his failure led to the vexation and illness that caused his death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Filled with grief at the news of Jewish success, the king fell into a desperate illness while traveling through Persia.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He died in the town of Tabis after falling from his chariot.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His repentance was viewed by many as motivated only by fear.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He appointed a regent for his young son, Eupator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The persecution under Antiochus lasted about six years and four months.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Engines of war and machines for throwing stones were used in the continued siege.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Large forces in the tens of thousands were deployed in the subsequent military actions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Bethsura was a key location to the south of Jerusalem.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas abandoned the siege of the castle on Sion to meet the enemy at a defile called Bethzacharam.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy used wine and the juice of mulberries to incite their elephants to fight. These beasts were often irritated by white or red colors and were sometimes given spirits to drink to increase their aggression.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Large numbers of men—sometimes thirty or even sixty—were known to fight from towers placed upon the backs of elephants. These massive animals could carry several thousand pounds with ease.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Trumpets were used to coordinate the various divisions and phalanxes of the army.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The elite troops and officers carried shields of gold or silver, reflecting the great magnificence of the rulers of that era.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Large numbers of both infantry and horsemen fell during the fierce battles in the vanguard.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Eleazar, the brother of Judas, performed a heroic feat by attacking a massive, richly adorned elephant that he believed carried the king. The skin of these animals is hard, except on the belly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Eleazar exposed himself to certain death for the sake of his religion and people, a noble and virtuous motive. Most commentators highly commend his fortitude and dedication.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He attacked the elephant in its most vulnerable part—the belly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas eventually retired to the fortified temple. The enemy took Bethsura after a long and costly siege, leaving only the land north of it still under Jewish control.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy used various engines of war and javelins, some filled with poison, to attack the Jewish fortifications.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Internal rivalries among the enemy commanders provided Judas a reprieve from his most imminent danger.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'giving of the right hand' was a sign of a most inviolable engagement among the ancient peoples of that region.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy had attempted to abolish the Jewish religion, but the people stood firm in their faith.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The young king was often under the influence of advisors who bore the greatest blame for the persecutions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy eventually broke their oath and pillaged the holy city.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Chronicle of the year 162 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 3843.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Demetrius I (Soter) escaped from Rome to claim his lawful throne. He eventually seized power and put the previous usurpers and their advisors to death.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Alcimus, a man from a less powerful priestly family, sought the high priesthood. However, the Jews did not recognize him because he had turned away from the Law under previous rulers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Renegades falsely accused Judas and his followers to the king, hoping to gain favor through gifts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A powerful governor was sent to Judea to support the usurping high priest and oversee the region.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 'scribes' served as both civil and military officers during this period.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Assideans—pious and learned men—were initially deceived by the false high priest, who cruelly murdered sixty of them.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The 78th Psalm is thought by many to refer to this cruel massacre.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After leaving a governor in charge, the enemy general returned to the king's presence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: False accusations continued against the people, aiming to destroy the resistance led by Judas Machabeus.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nicanor, a terrible enemy high in the king's favor, was sent to Judea. Though he attempted a false peace, Judas escaped his stratagems.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A notable site near Jerusalem where peace was briefly considered.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas was again forced to retreat to the temple area after suffering losses in battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jews maintained the laudable custom of praying for their rulers, a practice the Church continues today.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy general blasphemed in a way that 'grated the ears' of the faithful, who turned away in horror.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The final battle with the forces of Nicanor.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The decisive battle took place at Adasa in the land of Ephraim.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: References to ancient historical enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Priests used trumpets to give signals and coordinate the troops throughout the battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They surrounded the enemy with the wings of their army.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Nicanor was slain, and his defeat was displayed before Jerusalem as a sign of God's victory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This victory was commemorated for many years on the day before the feast of Purim.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While Judas attempted to settle religious matters, his enemies continued to prepare for war.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical timeline: around 188 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Chronicle of 189 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Scripture speaks with respect of the character of the ancient Romans—their friendship and devotion to the public good. The Jews were among the first to seek an alliance for their liberty.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas had heard of the Romans' fame, which often magnified the power of that distant people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Spain was famous in ancient times for its rich and productive mines.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The influence of the Romans eventually reached the most distant parts of the known world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Romans had subdued powerful neighboring kingdoms, such as the Macedonians.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Massive armies of that era sometimes included more than a hundred elephants.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Defeated monarchs were forced to pay enormous tributes and cede territories and resources to the Romans.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Roman influence extended even to nations near the Indus River.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Other powerful leagues and neighbors were eventually brought under their control.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Local kings ruled only by the favor and friendship of the Romans.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After the expulsion of their kings, the Romans established a stable republic.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Roman Senate, composed of hundreds of men, oversaw the affairs of the state.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas heard that the Romans were governed by one man at a time and were free from internal envy. While this was largely true in the early Republic, they were seen as being entirely bent on the public good.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Romans welcomed the alliance, especially as they expected future conflict with the Syrian monarchs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The agreement established that both nations would support each other in times of war.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Mutual agreements regarding support for each other's enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Self-preservation dictated that the Jews seek an alliance with others to defend against a king who intended to exterminate them. All nations have a right to seek redress under extreme oppression.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Chronicle of 161 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical events of 160 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 158 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Bacchides returned with his best troops before the Roman alliance could send assistance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the locations of the subsequent military campaign.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas was encamped with only a few thousand men against a massive enemy force.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Even the strongest leaders feel trouble in the face of such odds, yet trusting in God, they are assured of ultimate victory.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas chose to fight even with small numbers rather than face slavery and shame. He believed that God could save by many or by few.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy phalanx was a compact and powerful body of many thousands of men.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The site of a final, fierce military engagement.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas Machabeus died a glorious death in battle. He lived as a holy priest, a valiant soldier, and a religious prince dedicated to the glory of his people and his God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical note on the burial site at Modin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Only a portion of the many heroic actions of Judas are recorded in this account.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Without a ruler, a people often falls into great distress and confusion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemies abused and slew many of those who had been faithful to Judas.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: There had been no prophet in Israel for many generations since the return from captivity.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan succeeded his brother as the next leader and eventual high priest of the nation.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the location of Mizpah and the surrounding region.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy attempted to attack on the Sabbath, thinking the Jews would not defend themselves on that holy day.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan's brother was slain, and their group's baggage was seized by hostile neighbors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Strategic locations during the subsequent conflict.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Punishing faults with justice is a virtue. Sometimes people suffer for the faults of their leaders to prevent others from imitating such crimes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan and his men were forced to engage the enemy even on the Sabbath day to preserve their lives.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan and his men swam across the river to escape after a fierce battle in which they inflicted heavy losses on the enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Strategic towns in the land of Ephraim.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy maintained a garrison in the castle on Sion to keep the city under their control.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The usurping Alcimus attempted to remove the walls that separated the priests from the people, as if to abolish their holy distinction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the end of the false high priest's reign.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan dealt with those who betrayed his people to the enemy.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Strategic note on the desert fortresses.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan showed himself in the field with a large army to penetrate the enemy lines and show the nation's strength.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Evil counsel eventually proves detrimental to the one who gives it.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jonathan made good use of his alliances and began to lay foundations for national independence, acting like the ancient judges.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Events starting in 153 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Historical timeline: around 150 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Context of 150 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The year 148 B.C.

text: Jason introduced unholy Greek customs, such as gymnasiums where youth were corruptly encouraged to follow pagan fashion. He even sought to rename the people to please the king.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He sought the high priesthood through unholy means and bribes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Previous leaders had procured real advantages for the city through diplomatic skill.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He introduced emblems of pagan 'liberty' that led many away from the true Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This was the height of 'Hellenism'—the influx of foreign customs. Though of priestly descent, the usurper was not a true high priest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: When true religion is abandoned, it is replaced by vanity. Priests neglected their duties to compete in unholy games, such as throwing the discus.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: They sought corruptible crowns of glory while ignoring the rewards of heaven.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God eventually punished their betrayal by causing the very people they imitated to become their persecutors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some attempted to bring the unholy Olympic games to Jerusalem.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some deputies were ashamed to follow such orders and used the money instead for more appropriate public purposes.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The monarch pretended to defend his young nephew in Egypt but actually aimed to seize the kingdom for himself.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Large celebrations with lights and torches were standard ways to show public joy in the ancient world.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Menelaus usurped the high priesthood. Since he may not have been of the priestly tribe, his actions were doubly criminal to those who followed the Law.

text: Onias III retreated to an asylum to escape false accusations. Even the unholy king respected his merit and wept at his death—a rare moment of humanity from a cruel ruler.

author: Haydock (Revised)

text: Miraculous visions of fiery soldiers and horses were seen for many days—true signs of God's coming judgment, meant to move people to repentance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: False rumors of the king's death led many to rejoice, which only incured the monarch's wrath upon his return.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Civil war and unholy vows are always devastating for the welfare of a nation.

text: The Lacedemonians were believed by some to share an ancestral link with the Jews through Abraham.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In the chaos, some were refused even the basic burial rights normally granted to strangers.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The king judged the city to be in open revolt and took it with great violence, though some suggest his partisans simply opened the gates.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Tens of thousands were slain or sold into slavery during the unholy conquest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God sometimes allows His holy places to be profaned when the people's piety has failed. Rites are for God's glory, but when they cease to serve Him, the holy things may be removed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After victory, the king boasted with extreme pride, as if he could command the very forces of nature.

text: Note on the Athenian senator involved in the decrees.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The temple was unholily rededicated to pagan deities. To avoid persecution, some neighbors denied their link to the Jews and requested their own temples be dedicated to the Greek gods.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The temple area was filled with unholy practices that were strictly forbidden by the Law of Moses.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It became a criminal offense even to identify as a Jew, just as it was later dangerous to identify as a Christian.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: People were forced into monthly birthday celebrations for the king and made to follow unholy processions dedicated to pagan idols.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Neighboring nations were encouraged to assist in the suppression of the Jewish people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Four great martyrdoms are recorded: the women and their children, those keeping the Sabbath, the aged Eleazar, and the seven brothers with their mother.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The governor oversaw the unholy suppression in the city of Jerusalem.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A vital caution: God punishes His people for their correction and trial, rather than for their total destruction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Punishment can be a mercy, preventing a people from becoming completely incorrigible in their sins.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God corrects His children for their trial, a principle of Divine justice that pagan sages did not understand.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Eleazar, a scribe of great learning and fortitude, is regarded as a protomartyr of the Old Law. He suffered before the unholy king for his faith.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He chose to suffer torture rather than lead a criminal life by breaking the Law of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He spat out the unholy meat, refusing to save his life through a betrayal of his conscience.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His friends offered a 'wicked pity,' suggesting he pretend to comply while eating his own food. He refused, knowing that to feign consent to a false religion is never lawful and would scandalize the weak.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He chose to go to his grave with a clear conscience. Under the Old Law, the saints were detained until the time of the Messiah. He believed it was better to suffer than to commit a single sin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He believed that a virtuous life should end in a haven of peace rather than a shipwreck of old age.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He was certain of existence and reward after death, a truth that became more fully developed as the time of the Messiah approached.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some martyrs seem not to have felt their torments, as God sustained them and made their suffering no more than they could bear.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Timeline note: around 167 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The brothers were tortured with leather thongs and were later known as the 'Machabees,' a name made famous by Judas. While their names remain unknown, various Church Fathers have highly extolled their sacrifice and consult the large historical discourses on their history.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The phrase 'be comforted' implies that God will restore His servants to favor after a period of trial and chastisement.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jews maintained their native language (Hebrew or Syriac) even while learning others for commerce. This young man was addressed by his mother in her own tongue to strengthen his resolve.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Driven by Divine inspiration, the martyrs sometimes spoke harshly to their unholy persecutors. All of their responses clearly mention their belief in the coming resurrection of the dead. They were true 'Christians' in their deeds.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The answer regarding the source of power greatly resembles Christ’s own response to Pilate.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The mother witnessed the death of all seven of her sons, and her dedication to God remains perfect in the historical record.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The formation of a child in the womb remains a testament to the power of the Creator.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The unholy king feared the mother was insulting him in a language he did not understand. He attempted to bribe the last son with promises of earthly 'happiness'—something beyond the power of any monarch to truly provide.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: An oath to do evil must not be kept. The mother 'promised' to counsel her son, but not as the king wished; she exhorted him to choose eternal life over worldly honors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: It was common practice in that era for children to be nursed for three years before transitioning to solid food.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: God created the world from 'nothing,' not out of pre-existent or eternal matter.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: She encouraged her son to trust in God's mercy and to look toward an eternity of bliss.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The brothers 'fell under the covenant of God,' enduring a short period of labor to gain eternal life.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The mother’s prophecy that the king would face Divine judgment was eventually fulfilled.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The persecution was severe but short, as Judas began to liberate the nation by God’s mercy shortly thereafter.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some accounts suggest the mother ended her own life to avoid being defiled by the unholy, potentially guided by Divine inspiration.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Church has honored these 'martyrs of the resurrection' from the earliest ages. Their testimony regarding the afterlife is the most distinct recorded in that era.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas at this time operated from the deserts and began attacking the enemy from various villages and strongholds.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Prayer is especially necessary and powerful before entering into battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The blood of the unjustly slain cries out for justice; this differs from the blood of Christ, which speaks of mercy and forgiveness.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The unholy local governor found himself unable to contend with Judas after his initial victories.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Immense forces were deployed, though the historical record focuses on those engaged in the primary battles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The king was heavily in debt and was forced to raise money in his eastern provinces to pay his obligations.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy expected to capture many slaves at a set price, but their plans were eventually thwarted.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas and his men looked toward Divine vengeance and justice against their persecutors.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A just and religious cause is the best foundation for a successful military defensive.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Jewish force was relatively small and poorly armed, yet they were not discouraged by the odds.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: This refers to an earlier victory where Jewish forces helped repulse an invasion of Gauls (Galatians) in the east. These unholy invaders were powerful but were defeated by the valor of the Jews.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the companions and relatives of the leaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Before battle, the Law and other Scriptures were read to prepare the people and signal that victory would come from God’s help.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Thousands of the enemy fell on the field of battle and in the subsequent pursuit.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The pursuit was paused for the Sabbath, as the people wished to be in camp before the holy day.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Following the spirit of the Law, the spoils of victory were shared with widows and orphans as well as the troops.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Judas defeated another general in a separate engagement after the temple was purified.

author: Haydock (Revised)

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The monarch had previously claimed divine honors for himself, but he was soon shown to be a mere mortal.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: His 'repentance' was driven by fear and pain rather than true sorrow for his offenses. True repentance is more than just acknowledging punishment—it requires a turn toward God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He promised to change his policy and make Jerusalem a free and independent city.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He promised the Jews that they would enjoy the same privileges as the citizens of his own capital—a grant that had been neglected during the wars.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He even offered to pay for the holy sacrifices from his own royal revenues, following the example of previous monarchs.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: He addressed the people as 'citizens,' using a formal style intended to win favor and stability.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The monarchs of the east often took precautions regarding the succession. The king had previously attempted to plunder eastern temples but was repulsed.

text: His desperate and sudden promises were seen by many as a sign that he was becoming deranged.

text: The people struck fire from stones to rekindle the temple lamps. The Church continues similar practices of honoring sacred fire today.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The rededication was celebrated with ceremonies and green branches, symbols of the people's joy and restoration.

author: Haydock (Revised)

text: Even our Savior observed this feast (Hanukkah), confirming its historical and religious significance.

text: Some leaders were disgusted by the cruelties against the Jews, though taking one's own life is never true fortitude. True heroism is found in dedication to God.

text: God eventually punished the unholy designs of the hostile neighbors, as the heart is the true source of all sin.

text: The enemy general feared that if he were captured, he would face immediate and unmerciful judgment.

text: The temple dedicated to a pagan idol was destroyed during the successful conquest of the city.

text: Scythopolis, formerly known as Bethsan, was an ancient city located near the Lake of Tiberias.

text: The weeks of Pentecost were celebrated seven weeks after the initial Passover festival.

text: Strategic defenses were often established outside the main city walls for better regional security.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Following the Sabbath (on Sunday), the people gathered with their families to honor and honor those who had fallen in the conflict.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Some soldiers had taken unholy objects dedicated to idols from the enemy, contrary to the Law. The leader charitably hoped that their deaths had served as a chastisement and an expiation for their fault.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people believed that sins could be forgiven in the next world, especially through the intercession of the living. This belief in praying for the dead became a standard Jewish doctrine. Various Church Fathers also affirm that prayers can remit venial sins or mitigate the sufferings of the deceased in the afterlife.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A significant sum was sent for sacrifice on behalf of the deceased, firmly establishing the practice of praying for the dead within the Jewish tradition—a practice later adopted by the early Church and supported by various Apostles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Charity requires us to judge the dead with hope. The leader believed those who died fighting for a righteous cause might find mercy through ignorance or deathbed repentance.

text: The year 163 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The young king Eupator alongside his advisor came in person to the battle, hoping their presence would animate the troops for the campaign.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The size of the enemy army varied throughout the campaign as different detachments and reinforcements were integrated into the massive auxiliary force.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The unholy usurper Menelaus attempted to regain favor by pretending deep concern for the people’s welfare, though he sought only his own power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The unholy leaders who brought about these disturbances were eventually judged. One such official was conducted to Berea to face his final sentence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A terrible form of punishment was used: placing the criminal on a beam over a pit of ashes until they fell to their ruin.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The young king’s initial intentions were viewed as 'barbarous,' as he planned to treat the nation more harshly than his father had.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The traditional senate or council of elders was not convoked during these transitions of power.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The leader gave his men a pious watchword before their night attack, eventually capturing the enemy's quarter. Overseeing the war elephants was a prestigious military rank in the Syrian army.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The traitor was eventually discovered in the camp and dealt with accordingly.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The leader’s movements were swift and effective, reflecting the style of the most successful ancient military commanders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Despite acts of individual heroism, the situation moved toward a negotiated peace. Conditions were granted that would have been advantageous had the monarch not immediately ordered the city's defenses destroyed.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: A Jewish governor was eventually appointed over several frontier and strategic regions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Hostile neighboring groups raged against the new peace treaties, wishing to see the resistance suppressed or the truce broken.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Demetrius, the rightful heir to the crown, eventually returned from Rome to stake his claim to the Syrian throne.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Alcimus sought the high priesthood but was rejected by the faithful, having voluntarily defiled himself during the unholy persecution. His apostasy rendered him ineligible under the Law.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Lavish gifts in the form of golden boughs were often presented to the temple and were greatly admired for their craftsmanship.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The Assideans were the most zealous defenders of the Law. Alcimus used deceit and malice to frame them to the king as the source of national unrest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the general Nicanor and his role in the regional conflicts.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The pagans viewed the temple in Jerusalem as a site of profound regional significance.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Unholy politicians often advanced their careers by pillaging and suppressing those who remained faithful to their religion.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The leadership maintained a strategic and watchful silence to avoid any potential enemy traps.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The leader laid all proposed peace terms before the people for their collective input and consent.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: While some believed there was a sincere bond between the leader and his counterpart, others viewed it as a strategic move for stability.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the attempts by officials to ensure their own successors were maintained in office.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: One of the generals acted as a typical politician of the era, willing to sacrifice justice or religion for his own interest.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Initial military skirmishes resulted in significant and demoralizing losses for the enemy army.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people truthfully stated they did not know the leader's location when questioned under pressure by the enemy forces.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The unholy general threatened to rededicate the temple to a pagan god of wine if the leader was not surrendered to him.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: No crime could be found against the faithful people, save for their unwavering love for their country and the Law of God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people had history of being judged and persecuted for their refusal to adopt the unholy customs of the Gentiles.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The dramatic story of Razias' end is recorded in Scripture but not necessarily praised for imitation. While the Church generally condemns suicide, some writers suggest exceptional cases may involve a special Divine direction.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Note on the specific physical details and location of the final stand of Razias.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Temporal context: around 161 B.C.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy planned an attack on the Sabbath, hoping to exploit the people’s tender consciences. However, the leader was prepared, knowing the Law allowed for defensive actions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy general spoke with extreme pride, claiming his authority was superior to that of the Lord of Heaven.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The general planned to erect a public monument of conquest after his expected victory over the people.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people remained cautious because of the previous betrayals and unholy oaths of their regional enemies.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The leader’s ultimate confidence came from a supernatural dream or vision that he recognized as a gift and message from God.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: In this vision, Onias and Jeremiah appeared, demonstrating that the saints in the next world intercede for the living. This belief is consistent across both Jewish and Christian traditions.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: Jeremiah presented a golden sword to the leader, encourage him to defend the people. He recognized that while there is a time for peace, there is also a time for defensive war.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The leader exhorted his men to give their lives for their country and the sanctuary rather than trusting solely in the size of their army.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy invoked the help of their pagan idols through songs before the engagement began.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people experienced the Divine Presence and favor as they achieved a swift and decisive victory over the unholy invaders.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The enemy forces were routed even before they could fully prepare their armor and lines for battle.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people composed and sang canticles in their own language to celebrate God's deliverence.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The head of the defeated unholy general was displayed as a sign of his fall, and his arm was hung before the temple as a testimony.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The people raised their voices toward heaven, praising the Lord for making Himself manifest in their hour of need.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The feast was celebrated on the 13th of Adar, corresponding to February or March in our calendar.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: After the final persecutors were overcome, the land enjoyed a period of rest and restored order.

author: Haydock (Revised)
text: The author concludes by noting that whether his style is great or middling, he has done his best to summarize this great history. The purpose of this work was to counteract unholy influences and to show that the Word of God and the Church shall never pass away.
